positions.summary: No explicit healthcare policy statements found for this race. His general Libertarian stance favoring free markets and skepticism of government programs suggests opposition to expanded government-run healthcare, though he has not directly addressed this topic.

Cristo supports immigration among those seeking employment and entrepreneurship, opposing the border wall as wasteful government spending while also opposing taxpayer-funded welfare. He criticizes politicians who scapegoat immigrants while avoiding accountability for their own spending.
positions.notable_quote: Politicians tax people and then shift the blame onto immigrants.

positions.summary: No direct statements on gun policy found for this race, but as a committed Libertarian he aligns with the party's strong support for Second Amendment rights with opposition to restrictions, registration requirements, and gun control laws.

No explicit statements found on energy or environment. Libertarian philosophy generally favors deregulation and free-market approaches over government mandates on energy, suggesting opposition to regulatory environmental policy.
No public statements found on infrastructure or transportation policy. This issue was not addressed on his campaign website or in available interviews and columns.
No public statements found on land use or development policy at the statewide level.
Not applicable at this statewide level; no statements found on local community character or growth issues.

No explicit curriculum statements found. His Libertarian philosophy and his expressed opposition to government imposing values on individuals suggests alignment with parental rights over state-mandated curriculum, though he has not directly addressed this topic.
General rhetoric favoring decentralization and 'no-nonsense' honest government; no specific documented transparency, ethics, or elections proposals.
positions.notable_quote: Texas needs leadership that respects individual liberty, spends responsibly, and tells the truth.

key_endorsements: Libertarian Booster PAC (project of former Libertarian National Committee Executive Director Wes Benedict and Arthur DiBianca)
profiles.campaign_finance_notes: The Libertarian Booster PAC pledged $2,000 from its local fund and raised additional contributions to help Cristo meet Texas' $3,750 filing fee. Wes Benedict announced the PAC helped pay $22,500 in combined filing fees for six statewide Libertarian candidates including Cristo. No independent large-donor fundraising or Texas Ethics Commission filings were found in available sources. As a minor-party statewide candidate, Cristo is expected to have minimal campaign finance activity compared to major-party candidates.

profiles.bio_summary: Mayes Middleton is a 7th-generation Texan, oil and gas executive (Middleton Oil Company), attorney (UT Law School, licensed 2008), and current Texas State Senator from District 11 (Brazoria, Galveston, and Harris Counties). He previously served two terms in the Texas House of Representatives, where he chaired the Texas House Freedom Caucus, before winning a Senate seat in 2021. He won the May 2026 Republican primary runoff against U.S. Rep. Chip Roy, spending nearly $17 million of personal funds, and now faces Democrat Nathan Johnson in the general election.
profiles.overall_summary: Middleton positions himself as the ideological successor to Ken Paxton, running on a platform of aggressive border enforcement, culture-war priorities (anti-DEI, anti-transgender policies, election integrity), and strong alignment with President Trump and the America First agenda. He is one of the most consistently conservative members of the Texas Legislature by multiple scorecards. His limited courtroom experience — despite holding a law degree — has been a central line of attack from opponents. His campaign is almost entirely self-funded through his oil and gas fortune.
key_endorsements: Texas Right to Life PAC (prior legislative races)
profiles.campaign_finance_notes: Middleton self-funded nearly all of his campaign, spending approximately $16–17 million of personal funds from his oil and gas fortune — over 90% of total receipts. He raised $11.8 million in the second half of 2025 alone, with $5 million cash on hand heading into the runoff. He has also been a prolific donor to conservative causes over 15 years, contributing over $300,000 to Empower Texans PAC and funding roughly 23 of 24 initial House Freedom Caucus members. Campaign finance reports are filed with the Texas Ethics Commission.

positions.summary: Middleton supports eliminating government waste, fraud, and abuse through a 'DOGE Texas' initiative, and has a perfect or near-perfect score from Texans for Fiscal Responsibility. He rejects his own state pension and healthcare and donates his salary to charity.
positions.notable_quote: Public service, not self-service.

positions.summary: Middleton supports zero-tolerance policies for violent crime, stronger bond requirements for dangerous offenders, and holding 'rogue prosecutors' accountable. He frames the AG role as aggressively enforcing law and order.
positions.notable_quote: Zero-tolerance policies for violent crime and stronger bonds for dangerous offenders to keep criminals off our streets.

positions.summary: Middleton supports school choice and has backed conservative education funding structures through his legislative record. He authored a bill allowing school districts to employ chaplains as student counselors.

Middleton has voted for all major Texas abortion restrictions including the 6-week ban, medication abortion restrictions, and criminal penalties for providers. His campaign pledges to uphold and enforce pro-life laws as AG.
positions.notable_quote: Uphold pro-life laws.

positions.summary: No direct statements on social safety net programs, but his strongly conservative fiscal record and DOGE-style anti-spending messaging imply opposition to expansion of welfare and government assistance programs.

No direct campaign statements on Social Security or Medicare, but his strongly conservative fiscal philosophy and alignment with the national MAGA/America First agenda suggest skepticism of expansion.

Middleton authored the Save Women's Sports Act banning transgender athletes in collegiate women's sports, opposes 'gender ideology in schools,' and has consistently backed anti-LGBTQ+ legislation throughout his legislative career.
positions.notable_quote: Led the charge against radical woke policies like DEI, ESG, Critical Race Theory, and allowing men in women's sports.

positions.summary: Middleton authored and passed what he describes as the strongest ban on COVID vaccine mandates in the country, reflecting strong anti-mandate healthcare views. He rejects government-provided healthcare for himself.

Middleton pledges to sue sanctuary cities, aggressively enforce Trump deportation orders, and combat human trafficking as a top AG priority. He describes himself as fully aligned with the Trump border security agenda.
positions.notable_quote: Sue to stop 'sanctuary cities' from ignoring the law and harboring illegal criminals.

positions.summary: Middleton co-authored Texas's Constitutional Carry bill and supports Second Amendment protections without government fees or restrictions.

As president of an independent oil and gas company, Middleton strongly supports Texas energy independence, protecting oil and gas jobs, and pledges to fight federal environmental regulations and ESG investment mandates.
No direct positions found on infrastructure or transportation policy in the context of the AG race.
No direct positions found on land use or development policy in the context of the AG race.
Not directly relevant to a statewide AG race; no specific positions found.

Middleton strongly opposes gender ideology and Critical Race Theory in schools, supports parental rights in education, backed chaplains in schools, and has promoted prayer and Bible study access in public schools.

Middleton pledges to enforce open-records laws and pursue accountability for misuse of taxpayer funds via a 'DOGE Texas' AG initiative — a pro-enforcement transparency plank — but has no documented position on expanded ethics rules or voting access.

profiles.bio_summary: Nathan Matthew Johnson (born February 12, 1968, Fort Worth, TX) is a three-term Democratic Texas state senator representing District 16 (North Dallas) since 2018, when he became the first Democrat to win the seat in over three decades. He holds a B.S. in physics from the University of Arizona and a J.D. from UT School of Law, and practices commercial litigation at Thompson Coburn in Dallas. He won the May 2026 Democratic primary runoff with 60.5% of the vote and faces Republican Mayes Middleton in the November 2026 general election.
profiles.overall_summary: Johnson runs as a reform candidate focused on restoring the AG's office to its traditional functions — consumer protection, Medicaid fraud enforcement, child support collection, and government transparency — after what he characterizes as Ken Paxton's politicization of the office. His platform is notably institutionalist: he pledges independence from partisan agendas while also committing to sue the Trump administration where he believes federal actions violate the law. His Senate voting record aligns closely with the Texas Democratic caucus mainstream, with explicit progressive stances on reproductive rights, gun safety, and LGBTQ+ issues.
key_endorsements: Everytown for Gun Safety / Moms Demand Action
Asian Texans for Justice Action Fund
Former Dallas Mayor Ron Kirk (former U.S. Trade Representative)
Dallas Morning News editorial board (2024 primary)
profiles.campaign_finance_notes: Johnson raised approximately $650,000 in the second half of 2025 and entered 2026 with roughly $750,000 cash on hand, leading the Democratic field but significantly outpaced by Republican primary candidates who collectively raised millions. Campaign finance reports are filed with the Texas Ethics Commission. No major donor controversies have been reported; Johnson has explicitly pledged not to take direction from mega-donors.

positions.summary: Johnson worked in the Texas Senate to prevent business exploitation of taxpayers and promoted economic opportunity in underserved communities. He opposed Paxton's use of expensive outside counsel ($3,780/hour) and pledges to recruit in-house legal talent to reduce costs.

Johnson supports cooperative relationships between the AG's office and local district attorneys rather than antagonistic ones. He prioritizes prosecuting identity theft, cybercrime, Medicaid fraud, and human trafficking, and opposes expanding AG prosecutorial authority beyond constitutional limits.
positions.notable_quote: I'm going to use the office to do what it's supposed to do... make sure that everybody knows the rules and that everybody follows the rules.

positions.summary: No explicit AG-race positions on education funding found. Johnson's Senate record and endorsement by Texas AFT (teachers union) suggest support for public education funding, but this is not a primary focus of his AG campaign.

Johnson has an unambiguous pro-choice record. As a state senator he filed bills to legalize medication abortion in Texas, nullify the 1925 abortion prosecution statute, and prohibit abortion travel bans. He is the only AG candidate in the Democratic field who voted against every anti-abortion bill in the Texas Senate.
positions.notable_quote: Nathan Johnson has always protected the right to choose. And he always will.

positions.summary: Johnson was a leading advocate for Medicaid expansion in the Texas Senate and worked on measures to expand healthcare access to lower-income Texans, reflecting broad support for safety-net programs.

No explicit statements found on Social Security or Medicare in the context of the AG race. His Democratic affiliation and advocacy for Medicaid expansion imply support for these programs, but this is not a focus of his AG campaign.

Johnson has received endorsements from the Houston LGBTQ+ Political Caucus and Stonewall Democrats, and his Senate voting record aligns with Democratic support for LGBTQ+ protections. His AG priorities page frames the office as a defender of individual constitutional rights broadly.

Johnson was a leading advocate for Medicaid expansion and authored legislation on palliative care, fentanyl testing strips, and marketplace health insurance reform in the Texas Senate. He supports the AG's office enforcing consumer protections in the healthcare marketplace.

Johnson supports border security against drug smuggling and human trafficking but explicitly opposes unconstitutional 'shock and awe' enforcement tactics. He has stated ICE is placing constitutional rights 'under threat' and would challenge federal immigration overreach as AG.
positions.notable_quote: Secure the 1,254-mile Texas border against drug smuggling and human trafficking while rejecting unconstitutional 'shock and awe' tactics and protecting state sovereignty from federal overreach.

positions.summary: Johnson supports raising the minimum age for assault weapon purchases to 21, universal background checks, a 72-hour cooling-off period after firearm purchases, and red-flag-style temporary removal of firearms from dangerous individuals. He has endorsed being 'good with Fs on the NRA report card.' Everytown for Gun Safety and Moms Demand Action have endorsed him.
positions.notable_quote: I'm good with Fs on the NRA report card.

positions.summary: Johnson played a central Senate role in strengthening the Texas power grid after Winter Storm Uri and worked to preserve renewable energy resources within ERCOT market restructuring. Sierra Club endorsement implies strong environmental credibility, though no specific AG-race environmental litigation pledges were found.

No specific positions on infrastructure found in the AG race context. Johnson served on the Texas Senate Transportation Committee, suggesting familiarity with the issue, but no AG-specific commitments were identified.
No positions on land use or development found in the AG race context. This is not a primary focus of his campaign.
No positions on community character or local growth issues found. This is not a focus of a statewide AG race.

No explicit AG-race positions on curriculum or parental rights found. Johnson's endorsement by Texas AFT and his Senate voting record suggest alignment with mainstream Democratic positions opposing restrictive curriculum mandates, but no specific statements were found.

Johnson explicitly pledges consistent, unbiased application of open-records and transparency laws, criticizing Paxton's selective enforcement favoring Republican interests. He prioritizes government transparency as a core AG function and supports strengthening the AG's basic infrastructure for legal opinions and information requests.
positions.notable_quote: It's been so long since people thought of the attorney general's office as a place where they have an attorney, an elected official on their side.

profiles.bio_summary: Tom Oxford is a Beaumont, Texas attorney and perennial Libertarian Party candidate who earned his B.A. in Political Science from the University of Texas (1979) and his J.D. from the University of Houston Law School (1982). He is the managing attorney at Waldman Smallwood PC, where he practices personal injury, civil rights, and immigration law, and has previously served as a supervising attorney for a Catholic Charities immigration project and worked in nonprofit legal aid representing low-income Texans. Oxford has run multiple times for the Texas Supreme Court (2012, 2014, 2020, 2022) before seeking the Attorney General post in 2026.
profiles.overall_summary: Tom Oxford is a low-profile Libertarian candidate with a sparse campaign digital footprint and no dedicated 2026 campaign website identified. His policy positions are almost entirely inferred from Libertarian Party platform alignment and his professional background in civil rights and immigration law; he has not completed candidate questionnaires or given substantive interviews in the 2026 cycle. Voters seeking detailed policy positions will find very little publicly available information beyond his party affiliation and legal practice areas.
profiles.campaign_finance_notes: No significant campaign finance activity identified through the Texas Ethics Commission search. Oxford has historically run low-budget Libertarian campaigns across multiple statewide cycles. No filings were readily surfaced for the 2026 Attorney General race as of June 2026.

positions.summary: As a Libertarian, Oxford is presumed to favor lower taxes, reduced government spending, and limited state intervention in the economy. No specific statements on fiscal policy were found for the 2026 AG race.

Libertarian candidates typically favor criminal justice reform, reduced incarceration, ending the drug war, and protecting civil liberties against prosecutorial overreach — all consistent with Oxford's civil rights legal practice. No explicit AG-specific statements found.
Libertarian platform typically supports school choice, reduced government role in education funding, and parental control. No specific statements from Oxford found.

Libertarians generally support personal bodily autonomy, which would suggest a pro-choice lean, though the party has internal diversity on this issue. Oxford has made no public statements on reproductive rights.

Libertarian philosophy generally opposes government-run welfare programs in favor of private charity. Oxford's pro bono and legal aid work shows personal commitment to the less fortunate, but no statements on government safety net programs were found.
Libertarian platform typically favors transitioning away from or reducing Social Security and Medicare toward private alternatives. No statements from Oxford found.
Libertarians broadly support LGBTQ+ civil liberties and oppose government discrimination, consistent with their personal freedom philosophy. Oxford has made no explicit statements on this topic.
Libertarian platform favors free-market healthcare and opposes government mandates or expansion of public health programs. No specific statements from Oxford found.

Oxford actively practices immigration law and serves as supervising attorney for a Catholic Charities immigration legal aid project, suggesting sympathy toward immigrants and likely support for more open or humane immigration policies consistent with Libertarian platform positions.
Libertarians are strong Second Amendment supporters who oppose most gun regulations. No specific statements from Oxford were found, but party alignment strongly suggests a pro-gun position.

Libertarian platform generally favors free-market energy policy, opposes regulatory mandates, and relies on property rights rather than government intervention to address environmental concerns. No statements from Oxford found.
No information found on Oxford's positions regarding infrastructure or transportation policy.
No information found on Oxford's positions regarding land use or development policy.
No information found on Oxford's positions regarding local community or growth issues. As a statewide AG candidate, this topic is minimally relevant.
Libertarians typically support parental rights in education and oppose government-mandated curriculum. No specific statements from Oxford found.
No documented statements on transparency, ethics, or election administration found.

profiles.bio_summary: Donald "Don" Huffines is a fifth-generation Texan, businessman, and former state senator (SD-16, 2015-2019) who built his wealth through car dealerships and real estate development with his twin brother. He entered politics as a tea party insurgent, served one Senate term without accepting a salary or perks, and previously ran for governor in 2022 before winning the 2026 GOP comptroller primary with 57% of the vote against Abbott-backed rivals.
profiles.overall_summary: Huffines is a strongly conservative, self-funding candidate who ran on "DOGE-ing" Texas government — auditing waste, eliminating DEI, and directing savings toward property tax relief. His platform blends fiscal conservatism (abolish property taxes, repeal franchise tax, gold-backed currency) with hardline social positions (no abortion exceptions, undoing LGBTQ protections, aggressive anti-immigration accounting). He defeated the Abbott-backed incumbent and won a Trump endorsement, positioning himself as the most ideologically consistent conservative in the race.
profiles.campaign_finance_notes: Huffines is predominantly self-funded, spending millions of his personal wealth in the 2026 primary — mirroring his self-funded 2022 gubernatorial campaign. He has historically refused government salary, pension, or office perks while in public office. His financial dominance overwhelmed Abbott-backed opponent Kelly Hancock in the primary. Texas Ethics Commission filings would contain full details; Transparency USA profile was inaccessible (403 error).

positions.summary: Huffines supports eliminating property taxes, repealing the state franchise tax on businesses, offsetting revenue with consumption taxes, and directing government waste savings into property tax relief. He scored 97% on the Texans for Fiscal Responsibility Index during his Senate tenure.
positions.notable_quote: I will DOGE Texas by exposing waste, fraud, and abuse in government to increase efficiency and put every penny we save into property tax relief.

positions.summary: No explicit criminal justice positions found for this campaign. His strongly conservative profile and support for open carry suggest a law-and-order, tough-on-crime stance, but no direct statements were identified.

Huffines is a strong advocate for school vouchers (taxpayer-funded private school stipends) and has pledged to use the comptroller's office to market the $1 billion voucher program. He supports market competition as the mechanism to improve public schools.
positions.notable_quote: What is the best solution to make our government schools the best they can possibly be? It's competition, of course.

positions.summary: Huffines opposes abortion in all cases with no stated exceptions.
Huffines has pledged that zero tax dollars will go to illegal aliens, terrorists, or 'woke ideology,' and frames government spending on social programs as waste to be audited and cut.

No positions identified on Social Security or Medicare. As a state-level office, these federal programs fall outside the comptroller's direct jurisdiction, and Huffines has not publicly addressed them in this campaign.
Huffines has proposed undoing local LGBTQ protections and eliminating DEI programs from state government.
positions.notable_quote: If you want a comptroller who will eliminate woke DEI from government...

positions.summary: No specific healthcare access positions were identified for this campaign. His general anti-government-spending posture and opposition to 'woke ideology' funding suggest opposition to expanded public health programs, but no direct statements were found.

Huffines has pledged to conduct a comprehensive accounting of illegal immigration costs to Texas across schools, health services, incarceration, and border security, and vows no tax dollars will go to illegal aliens.
positions.notable_quote: I'm gonna do an accounting of that...It's really not an equitable situation.

positions.summary: Huffines supported open carry legislation during his time in the Texas Senate.

No direct environmental policy statements found for this campaign. His platform to implement gold/silver-backed currency through the Texas Bullion Depository and keep government lean suggests alignment with fossil-fuel-friendly, deregulatory energy policy consistent with his broader conservative profile.

His prior record (anti-toll-road campaigning, eliminating vehicle inspections) centered on cutting government costs; no positions found on water supply or grid investment. A market-first lean is inferred from his strong anti-spending posture.
No positions identified on land use or development in the context of this statewide campaign. His background in real estate development may be relevant but no policy statements were found.
No positions identified. This topic is primarily a local/municipal concern and falls outside the scope of the state comptroller race.

Huffines supports parental school choice through vouchers and has historically supported teaching creationism in public schools. He frames parental rights in education as a core conservative priority.

Huffines explicitly pledges honest, transparent budgets and audits exposing wasteful spending (proactive-publication side), but his documented history of demanding 2020 election audits and stricter election-security measures places him on the security side of the voting-access dimension.
positions.notable_quote: Demand honest transparent budgets.

profiles.bio_summary: Sarah Eckhardt is a third-term Texas State Senator (District 14, Travis County) and the daughter of U.S. Congressman Bob Eckhardt. She was the first woman elected Travis County Judge (2015-2021), previously serving as a two-term county commissioner and county prosecutor, giving her over 20 years of government experience. She won the 2026 Democratic primary for Texas Comptroller on March 3, 2026, after briefly running for the 10th Congressional District seat before switching races in December 2025.
profiles.overall_summary: Eckhardt is running on a platform of government accountability, fiscal transparency, and ending no-bid contracts, framing the comptroller's role as an independent watchdog over the state's $337 billion budget. Her campaign emphasizes applying rigorous oversight to programs like the school voucher scheme, lowering costs for everyday Texans, and fighting what she characterizes as corruption and single-party entrenchment. Her Texas Senate record reflects strongly progressive positions on healthcare, education funding, reproductive rights, criminal justice, gun safety, and environmental policy, making her one of the most left-leaning candidates in statewide Texas politics in 2026.
profiles.campaign_finance_notes: Campaign accepts donations via ActBlue. Texas Ethics Commission filings are the primary source for state-level finance data; specific totals were not publicly available in accessible sources at time of research. Eckhardt entered the comptroller race late (December 8, 2025 — last day to file), which likely limited early fundraising compared to candidates who announced earlier. No notable large donor controversies were found in available sources.

positions.summary: Eckhardt supports converting homestead exemptions to flat-rate systems to benefit lower-income homeowners, phasing out fossil fuel tax subsidies, legalizing and taxing marijuana, and opposing corporate tax breaks that do not serve core government missions. As comptroller, she would focus on auditing the voucher program and eliminating no-bid contracts.
positions.notable_quote: We don't have time for funny business and sweetheart deals that benefit the powerful.

positions.summary: Eckhardt emphasizes addressing systemic racism and over-policing, and supports redirecting public investment from policing toward education, healthcare, and job programs. She voted against legislation she viewed as reducing gun transparency in Texas foster homes.

Eckhardt strongly opposes efforts to defund public education and supports increased state investment in K-12, higher education, and workforce training. As a state senator she voted yes on bills increasing teacher pay and special education funding. As comptroller she has pledged to audit the school voucher program.

Eckhardt is a vocal supporter of reproductive rights, calling Texas's abortion ban 'Zealously Cruel' and sponsoring SJR 21, a constitutional amendment to safeguard the right to reproductive autonomy. She advocates for restoration of safe and legal reproductive healthcare in Texas.

Eckhardt supports streamlining enrollment for Medicaid, SNAP, and CHIP programs and expanding access to social services. Her platform calls for deeper state investment in affordable housing and public health infrastructure.

No explicit state-level positions found on Social Security or Medicare, which are primarily federal programs. Her support for Medicaid expansion implies alignment with expanding public health coverage broadly.

Eckhardt co-sponsored SB 150 to prohibit discrimination based on sexual orientation and gender identity, and co-filed SB 168 to ban conversion therapy on minors. She received endorsement from the Houston LGBTQ+ Political Caucus.

Eckhardt is a strong advocate for Medicaid expansion to cover over 1 million additional Texans, particularly to keep rural hospitals open. She supports expanded telehealth access and opposes government intrusion into the doctor-patient relationship.

No specific immigration policy statements were found in her comptroller campaign materials. As a Democratic state senator she visited the border in March 2025, but detailed immigration voting records or stances were not available in accessible sources.

Eckhardt co-sponsored SB 370 requiring background checks for private gun sales and voted against legislation that would have reduced gun transparency in Texas foster homes. Her record reflects support for gun safety measures.

Eckhardt is a strong environmental advocate who supports phasing out fossil fuel tax subsidies, increasing energy efficiency standards, and community climate resilience planning. She was endorsed by Environment Texas and backed SB 788 creating an Office of Environmental Justice. As Travis County Judge she implemented the first PACE program in Texas for solar and energy efficiency financing.

Eckhardt supports statewide investment in transportation, electricity grid reliability, broadband, and water infrastructure. She backed SB 282 (Transportation Project Fund) and advocates for broadband expansion to support telehealth and economic opportunity.

Eckhardt supports affordable housing investment as part of her broader infrastructure platform. No detailed positions on specific land use or development regulations were found in her comptroller campaign materials.
Eckhardt's background as Travis County Judge and county commissioner reflects experience with local growth management, but this topic is not directly addressed in her statewide comptroller campaign.

Eckhardt opposes what she characterizes as attacks on public education and has pledged to audit the school voucher program as comptroller. Her opposition to vouchers implies concern about diversion of public funds from public schools, but she has not specifically addressed curriculum content standards in her comptroller campaign.

Transparency and government accountability are the central themes of Eckhardt's comptroller campaign. She wants to restore independent oversight of the state budget, eliminate no-bid contracts, and ensure the comptroller's office exposes state leaders who 'refuse to play by the rules.'
positions.notable_quote: The comptroller's office has not been using its full power for the benefit of the people of Texas.

profiles.bio_summary: V. Alonzo Echevarria-Garza is a municipal finance professional who serves as city manager of Hearne, Texas, having previously worked as Hearne's chief financial officer and in finance roles for the cities of Big Spring and Port Isabel. He is the Libertarian Party of Texas nominee for Comptroller of Public Accounts in 2026, having previously run for the same office in 2022, where he received approximately 211,542 votes (2.66% of the total). No campaign website with policy content, no responses to voter questionnaires, and no news interviews covering his political positions have been located by researchers or journalists.
profiles.overall_summary: Echevarria-Garza is a repeat Libertarian candidate with relevant municipal finance experience but an almost entirely absent public campaign presence. Journalists at Nexstar reported being unable to find a campaign website or contact information during his 2022 run, and his personal website (alonzoechavarria.com) contains only philosophical and personal content with no policy positions. All issue scores below are inferred from Libertarian Party of Texas platform positions, not from any direct statements by the candidate. Voters seeking his specific positions on comptroller-relevant issues such as tax policy, state spending, and fiscal transparency will find no publicly available information.
profiles.campaign_finance_notes: No Texas Ethics Commission campaign finance filings were located through public search. During his 2022 campaign, media outlets were unable to find a campaign website or contact information, suggesting minimal or no formal campaign infrastructure. No campaign finance data is publicly available for the 2026 race at this time.

positions.summary: As a Libertarian, Echevarria-Garza is presumed to favor reduced government spending, lower taxes, and limited redistribution of wealth. The Libertarian Party of Texas platform explicitly opposes government efforts to redistribute wealth and favors free markets. No candidate-specific statements on tax or comptroller-specific fiscal policy have been found.

The Libertarian Party generally favors criminal justice reform, reduced incarceration, and civil liberties protections, which pulls away from both standard conservative and progressive positions. No candidate-specific statements found.

Libertarians typically favor reduced government involvement in education funding and support school choice, vouchers, and privatization over increased public school spending. No candidate-specific statements found.

The Libertarian Party platform generally supports personal bodily autonomy, which would imply a pro-choice stance, though individual Libertarian candidates vary. No candidate-specific statements found.

Libertarians strongly oppose government wealth redistribution programs, including welfare, food assistance, and housing assistance, favoring voluntary charitable alternatives. No candidate-specific statements found.

The Libertarian Party opposes mandatory government retirement and healthcare programs, generally favoring privatization or phase-out of Social Security and Medicare. No candidate-specific statements found.

The Libertarian Party's civil liberties platform generally supports equal legal treatment regardless of sexual orientation or gender identity, opposing government discrimination. No candidate-specific statements found.
Libertarians oppose government-managed or mandated healthcare and favor free-market solutions. No candidate-specific statements found.
The Libertarian Party generally favors more open immigration, opposing restrictive border enforcement and deportation programs. No candidate-specific statements found.
Libertarians strongly support Second Amendment rights and oppose gun control measures. No candidate-specific statements found.

Libertarians generally oppose government environmental regulations and subsidies for energy, favoring market-based approaches; however, they also oppose corporate welfare to fossil fuel industries. No candidate-specific statements found.
Libertarians favor privatization of infrastructure and transportation over government spending programs. No candidate-specific statements found.
Libertarians generally oppose zoning regulations and government land-use restrictions, favoring property rights. No candidate-specific statements found.
No information available on local community growth positions. As a statewide candidate, this topic is outside the primary scope of the comptroller race.

Libertarians typically support parental rights in education and oppose government curriculum mandates, though this is outside the primary scope of the comptroller's office. No candidate-specific statements found.
No documented public statements on transparency, ethics, or elections policy found; city-manager experience alone is not a position.

profiles.bio_summary: Shehla Faizi is a Muslim American of Pakistani heritage who was born in the U.S., raised in Karachi, Pakistan, and has lived in Texas since 2012. She holds a Bachelor's degree in Graphic Design and a Master's degree in Strategic Communication, with a professional background in advertising, marketing journalism, and podcasting. Her political activism intensified after the 2016 election and the 2023 Gaza conflict, leading her to run as a Green Party candidate after losing confidence in both major parties.
profiles.overall_summary: Faizi is running a protest-oriented campaign focused on fiscal transparency, redirecting Texas tax revenue away from corporate interests and overseas military funding, and back to ordinary Texans. Her platform centers on the comptroller's core functions — tax spending transparency, state investment policy, and contract equity — while also taking positions on anti-BDS laws, ICE cooperation, and school vouchers. As a third-party candidate in a statewide race, she faces significant structural barriers and her campaign appears to be operating at a grassroots, low-budget level with no major endorsements identified.
key_endorsements: Green Party of Texas (nominated at convention, April 11, 2026)
profiles.campaign_finance_notes: No significant campaign finance filings were identified through Texas Ethics Commission searches. The campaign accepts donations via Donorbox (donorbox.org/faizifortexas), suggesting a small-donor grassroots fundraising model. No PAC funding or major donor support was identified, consistent with the Green Party's anti-corporate-PAC stance.

positions.summary: Faizi advocates for redirecting Texas tax revenue away from corporate tax breaks (specifically opposing data center tax incentives) and overseas expenditures, toward services for ordinary Texans. She supports full spending transparency so citizens can track how their tax dollars are allocated.
positions.notable_quote: Texas taxes should serve Texans first and foremost. We the people should no longer be viewed as an after-thought.

positions.summary: Faizi has stated she wants to keep local sheriffs independent from ICE, suggesting a preference for limiting collaboration between local law enforcement and federal immigration enforcement, which aligns with a more progressive public safety approach.
positions.notable_quote: Texas Sheriffs for Texans

positions.summary: Faizi has called for 'unbiased' school voucher policies and equitable state contract allocation, suggesting concern that current voucher programs may be administered in a way that advantages certain populations. No explicit stance on overall education funding levels was found.

No explicit position found on reproductive rights. As a Green Party candidate, a strongly pro-choice position is inferred from party platform alignment, but Faizi has not publicly addressed this topic in the context of this campaign.

Faizi has cited stagnant wages and the financial struggles of average Texans as motivating factors, and frames her candidacy as ensuring state investments serve working people. Expanded social safety nets are implied but not detailed.

No explicit position found on Social Security or Medicare. Green Party alignment suggests support for strengthening these programs, but the Texas Comptroller's office has limited direct jurisdiction over federal programs and Faizi has not addressed this topic.

No explicit position found. Green Party platform strongly supports LGBTQ+ rights, and this position is inferred from party alignment. Faizi has not publicly addressed LGBTQ+ issues in the context of this race.

No explicit position found on healthcare access. Green Party traditionally supports universal healthcare; Faizi's broader populist framing around making government work for ordinary people implies support for expanded healthcare access, but she has not specifically addressed this topic.

Faizi explicitly opposes local law enforcement cooperation with ICE under her 'Texas Sheriffs for Texans' platform plank, arguing local sheriffs should remain independent from federal immigration enforcement.
No explicit position found on gun policy. Green Party alignment suggests support for stricter gun regulations, but Faizi has not addressed this topic in the context of this campaign.

No explicit position found specific to energy or environmental policy. The Green Party's core identity centers on environmental protection and clean energy transition, so a strongly progressive position on this topic is inferred from party alignment. Faizi has not detailed these positions for this race.

No explicit position found on infrastructure or transportation. Faizi's broader emphasis on redirecting state investment toward ordinary Texans implies support for public infrastructure, but no specific positions were identified.
No position found on land use or development policy. This topic was not addressed in any available campaign materials.
No position found on community character or local growth issues. As a statewide candidate, this topic was not addressed in available campaign materials.
No position found on education curriculum or parental rights. Faizi mentioned school vouchers in the context of equitable administration but did not address curriculum issues.

Transparency and accountability are central to Faizi's campaign. She explicitly pledges to create tax spending transparency so citizens can track how their dollars are allocated and to implement checks and balances in the Comptroller's office.
positions.notable_quote: After seeing both Democrats and Republicans sell out to donors and foreign lobbyists, I've had it with politics as usual.

profiles.bio_summary: Dr. Dawn Buckingham (born 1968) is an oculoplastic surgeon and Republican politician who became Texas's first female Land Commissioner in January 2023 after winning the 2022 election. She previously served in the Texas State Senate (2017-2023) representing District 24, where she earned a 96% lifetime rating from the American Conservative Union and was ranked among the most conservative senators in the chamber. A ninth-generation Texan and UT-Austin/UTMB Galveston graduate, she is running for re-election in November 2026 against Democrat Benjamin Flores.
profiles.overall_summary: Buckingham governs the GLO with a strongly conservative, pro-energy, pro-border-enforcement profile. Her tenure has focused on oil and gas revenue for public school funding, aggressive state border actions, Alamo heritage preservation, disaster relief administration, and veteran services. She has drawn controversy over Hurricane Harvey relief equity issues and her intervention in Alamo Trust communications around Indigenous Peoples Day. She holds endorsements from President Trump, major Texas Republican leaders, Gun Owners of America, Texas Oil and Gas Association, and the National Border Patrol Council, and entered the 2026 general election cycle with approximately $2.8 million cash on hand.
profiles.campaign_finance_notes: As of February 21, 2026, Buckingham reported $2,814,830 cash on hand, $1,764,864 in total contributions, and $887,422 in total expenditures for the 2026 cycle (Texas Ethics Commission filings via Transparency USA). She is well-funded relative to the race. Major organizational donors include the Texas Oil & Gas Association and affiliated industry groups. No significant campaign finance controversies have been reported.

positions.summary: Buckingham advocates for a low-tax, pro-business economy with limited government principles. As Land Commissioner, she emphasizes generating oil and gas royalty revenue for the Permanent School Fund as an alternative to tax-based school funding.
positions.notable_quote: Protecting Texas's 2.5 million oil and gas jobs and opposing federal overreach.

positions.summary: Buckingham launched the Jocelyn Initiative using GLO state land to support deportation and detention of undocumented immigrants she characterizes as criminal. She endorsed and received endorsements from law enforcement including the National Border Patrol Council and Houston Police Officers Union.

Buckingham prioritizes the GLO's role in funding public education through Permanent School Fund oil and gas revenues, claiming nearly $6 billion contributed during her tenure. She opposes federal interference in energy markets she argues harms school funding.
positions.notable_quote: Biden's Energy Export Ban Stealing Money from Texas Schoolchildren.

positions.summary: Buckingham co-authored the Texas Heartbeat Act (SB 8) banning abortion after approximately six weeks, and supported the trigger bill making abortion fully illegal if Roe v. Wade were overturned. She has stated she believes abortion should never be allowed unless the mother's life is in jeopardy.

No explicit statements on welfare, food assistance, or housing assistance programs found in the context of her current GLO role. Her overall limited-government, low-tax philosophy suggests skepticism toward expanding social safety net programs.

No explicit statements on Social Security or Medicare found in her GLO-focused campaign materials. Her American Conservative Union 96% lifetime rating and overall conservative record suggest alignment with reducing federal entitlement spending.

No direct statements on LGBTQ+ rights found in her current campaign. Her endorsements from Texas Values Action and other social conservative organizations, combined with her ACU 96% rating during her Senate tenure, strongly imply opposition to LGBTQ+ protections.

As a physician herself, Buckingham has not made healthcare access a central GLO issue. Her Senate record and conservative ideology suggest opposition to government-expanded healthcare, though the GLO role does not directly intersect with this policy area.

Buckingham has made border enforcement a signature GLO initiative, purchasing land for border wall construction, declaring Rio Grande islands sovereign Texas territory to combat cartel activity, and launching the Jocelyn Initiative to provide state land for federal deportation facilities.

Buckingham received endorsement from Gun Owners of America and supports Second Amendment rights. Her Senate record included pro-gun votes and she has consistently characterized herself as a Second Amendment defender.

Buckingham is a strong advocate for Texas oil and gas production, suing the Biden administration over LNG export restrictions, and framing fossil fuel revenue as essential to school funding and state independence. She supports carbon sequestration and emerging clean technologies as complements to, not replacements for, oil and gas.
positions.notable_quote: Protecting Texas's 2.5 million oil and gas jobs and opposing federal overreach.

positions.summary: Buckingham has overseen $9 billion in disaster recovery funding and $1 billion in flood mitigation, which includes infrastructure rebuilding. She has also pursued orphan oil well plugging and derelict vessel removal programs. No explicit stance on broader transportation infrastructure found.

As Land Commissioner, Buckingham has actively managed state-owned land for energy production, border security, and school fund revenue. She acquired land for border wall easements and offered state land for federal deportation facilities, reflecting a strongly development-friendly and security-oriented approach to state lands.

This topic is not directly relevant to her statewide GLO role. Her opposition to what she calls 'woke' reinterpretations of Texas history at the Alamo reflects a broadly traditionalist stance on community character and heritage.

positions.topic_name: Education Curriculum & Parental Rights (LISD / school board)
positions.summary: Buckingham previously served on the Lake Travis ISD school board and her GLO oversight of the Alamo museum's historical narrative pushing back against acknowledgments of slavery and Indigenous history signals strong alignment with parental-rights and traditional-curriculum conservative positions.
positions.notable_quote: Woke has no place at the Alamo.

positions.summary: No documented platform position on transparency, ethics rules, or elections. Her dismissal of criticism over GLO disaster-fund decisions suggests limited receptivity to external oversight, but this falls short of a documented policy stance.

profiles.bio_summary: Benjamin Flores is a Mexican-born immigrant who came to the United States in 1996 and built a 30-year career in cybersecurity and compliance. He currently serves on the Bay City City Council (elected 2023), operates a 10-acre heritage-breed pig farm with his family, and represents Bay City on the Houston-Galveston Area Council. He won the Democratic primary on March 3, 2026, defeating Jose Loya, and will face Republican incumbent Dawn Buckingham in the November 2026 general election. In June 2026, Flores announced a leukemia diagnosis; he is remaining in the General Land Office race and will undergo treatment at MD Anderson in Houston while off the campaign trail for several months.
profiles.overall_summary: Flores is a progressive Democrat whose platform centers on maximizing Permanent School Fund returns for public education, expanding renewable energy leasing on state lands, strengthening coastal climate resilience, and ensuring equitable disaster recovery. He is sharply critical of the current GLO's use of state resources for border and immigration enforcement. His positions on social issues (LGBTQ+ veterans' benefits, opposition to guest worker programs without citizenship pathways) and energy (welcoming renewables alongside oil and gas) reflect a solidly left-of-center profile for a statewide race. Campaign fundraising is modest, with under $70,000 cash on hand as of early 2026, though he holds a wide array of Democratic organizational endorsements.
key_endorsements: Roberto Alonzo, Democratic National Committee Member
Celina Vasquez, Democratic National Committee Member
Danny Tejada, former Mayor of Floresville
Carlos Aguilar III, former El Paso County Commissioner
profiles.campaign_finance_notes: Per Texas Tribune reporting (early 2026), Flores had approximately $67,720 cash on hand — a modest sum for a statewide race. Notable expenditures include $13,750 in-kind to Bill Naso Strategic Messages and $2,500 to Campaign Strategy Services. No significant large-dollar donors were identified. Texas Ethics Commission filings govern this race. His fundraising significantly trails incumbent Dawn Buckingham's war chest.

positions.summary: Flores wants to maximize professional investment management of the $56 billion Permanent School Fund, projecting up to $825 million more annually for schools, which he argues would allow property tax relief for Texans. He frames fiscal stewardship as enabling lower property taxes through better fund management rather than cutting spending.
positions.notable_quote: Increase GLO revenue to fund public schools and lower property taxes.

positions.summary: No direct criminal justice positions found. His opposition to using GLO state lands for immigration detention or enforcement activities suggests a progressive lean on civil liberties and due process, but no explicit criminal justice platform exists.

Flores strongly opposes school vouchers, stating the Permanent School Fund must not be redirected toward voucher schemes, and calls maximizing public school funding the GLO's core constitutional mission. He supports professional asset diversification of the fund to generate maximum revenue for public schools.
positions.notable_quote: Protect the Permanent School Fund from being redirected toward voucher schemes.

positions.summary: Flores states that 'medical decisions should remain between patients and doctors,' a commonly used progressive framing in the context of abortion rights, though the GLO has limited direct jurisdiction over reproductive policy. No explicit abortion position was found in campaign materials.

Flores emphasizes equitable distribution of disaster recovery funds to underserved and low-income communities, including multilingual outreach and public dashboards to track fund allocation. He frames GLO disaster recovery as a social equity issue, prioritizing those most in need.

No direct Social Security or Medicare positions found. As a state-level land office candidate, this topic falls outside his direct jurisdiction, but his support for healthcare as a right implies a progressive orientation.

Flores explicitly commits to guaranteeing full access to Veterans Land Board benefits for LGBTQ+ veterans without discrimination, stating there will be 'no asterisks, no second-class treatment.' He is endorsed by Stonewall Dallas.
positions.notable_quote: No asterisks. No second-class treatment.

positions.summary: Flores states he supports healthcare as a right and that medical decisions should remain between patients and doctors. He also supports THC and psilocybin research for therapeutic use, and supports broadband internet access as a right.

Flores opposes guest worker programs that lack citizenship pathways and explicitly opposes the GLO using state lands for detention or enforcement activities that violate due process. He switched from a gubernatorial race to the GLO race specifically to oppose how the current commissioner has used GLO resources for border and immigration enforcement.
positions.notable_quote: Prevent state lands from being used for detention or enforcement activities violating due process.

positions.summary: No gun policy positions found in campaign materials. As a Democrat running statewide in Texas, a moderate-to-progressive lean is inferred, but this issue is outside the direct GLO mandate and Flores has not addressed it publicly in this race.

Flores accepts climate change as real and urgent, particularly for Texas' 367-mile coastline, and plans to invite solar and wind energy producers to operate on state lands alongside oil and gas to increase grid capacity and school fund revenue. He frames renewable energy expansion and fossil fuel coexistence as complementary revenue strategies rather than in opposition.
positions.notable_quote: Welcome renewable energy producers alongside oil and gas, with more revenue for the Permanent School Fund meaning more money flowing to school districts.

positions.summary: Flores supports broadband internet access as a right and highlights infrastructure gaps as part of Texas' climate resilience challenge. He also advocates for lobbying to establish a VA Hospital in the Rio Grande Valley, reflecting a commitment to underserved infrastructure.

As the core GLO issue, Flores advocates for intelligent, data-driven state land management that maximizes revenue for schools and veterans while preserving long-term land values. He proposes expanding renewable energy leasing and ensuring equitable use of state lands, opposing their use for immigration enforcement purposes.

No direct positions on local community character or growth found. Flores's emphasis on equitable disaster recovery, multilingual outreach, and protecting underserved communities implies a progressive orientation toward inclusive community development.

No direct positions on curriculum or parental rights found. This issue falls outside the GLO's mandate. His strong opposition to vouchers implies opposition to the broader conservative parental-rights-in-education movement, but no explicit stance was stated.

Flores explicitly prioritizes transparency as a core governance value, pledging 'no nonsense in the General Land Office' and proposing public dashboards to track disaster fund allocation and speed. He frames his cybersecurity and compliance background as qualifications for rooting out inefficiency and political favoritism.
positions.notable_quote: No nonsense in the General Land Office.

profiles.bio_summary: Nathan "Nate" Sheets is a fifth-generation Texan, U.S. Navy veteran (1988–1994), and entrepreneur who built Nature Nate's Honey into the top-branded honey company in America before selling it in 2021. He is a committed conservative Christian who previously served in full-time ministry and on boards of faith-based nonprofits, and he ranches 10,000 acres in Texas. He defeated three-term Republican incumbent Sid Miller in the March 2026 primary with backing from Governor Greg Abbott, the Texas Farm Bureau, and major agricultural industry groups, and now faces Democrat Clayton Tucker in the November 2026 general election.
profiles.overall_summary: Sheets is a strongly conservative, faith-driven outsider candidate who leveraged his agricultural business credentials and reform messaging to unseat a long-tenured incumbent. His platform centers on clean food, reducing farmer fees, combating agroterrorism from hostile nations, supporting the Trump agenda on borders and immigration, and promoting MAHA (Make America Healthy Again) principles. While his office primarily covers agriculture, he has taken explicit conservative positions across a wide range of social and policy issues including abortion, gun rights, immigration, energy, and education.
key_endorsements: Former Texas Agriculture Commissioner Susan Combs
profiles.campaign_finance_notes: Sheets reported over $410,000 cash on hand in his first campaign finance report per the Texas Ethics Commission. Major donors include Mattress Mack ($10,000), Sen. Kevin Sparks and family ($13,000), and the Saulsbury Family ($25,000). By comparison, incumbent Sid Miller had only $37,000 on hand heading into the primary, reflecting Sheets' strong fundraising advantage.

positions.summary: Sheets opposes minimum wage increases, plans to implement zero-based budgeting and full audits at the Agriculture Department, and has promised to cut fees imposed on farmers and ranchers by the previous commissioner. He supports reducing government spending and regulatory costs on agriculture.
positions.notable_quote: Eliminating agriculture department fees and implementing zero-based budgeting with full audits.

positions.summary: Sheets prioritizes public safety and victim-centered justice and has expressed support for holding criminals accountable. His coalition outreach explicitly includes public safety advocates.
Sheets supports eliminating the U.S. Department of Education and returning control to states and local communities. He opposes foreign government funding in American schools.
positions.notable_quote: Eliminating the U.S. Department of Education and returning control to states and local communities.

positions.summary: Sheets strongly opposes abortion, describing himself as the most pro-life agriculture commissioner in Texas history. He supports protection of life from conception through natural death and enforcement of the Comstock Act restricting abortion-inducing drug transportation.
positions.notable_quote: Nate Sheets will be the most Pro-Life and Pro-Family Agriculture Commissioner in the history of Texas.

positions.summary: No direct statements on welfare or housing assistance were found, but his strong fiscal conservatism, opposition to minimum wage increases, and alignment with Republican orthodoxy strongly suggest opposition to expanding social safety net programs.

Sheets supports retaining existing Medicaid and Medicare programs but opposes creating additional taxpayer-funded healthcare programs. He also opposes government medical condition databases on privacy grounds.

Sheets opposes gender transition procedures for minors, characterizing them as 'child abuse,' and supports a traditional definition of marriage. His faith-based identity and conservative Christian values are central to his campaign.

Sheets aligns with the MAHA (Make America Healthy Again) movement, focusing on clean food and eliminating harmful chemicals as a public health approach. He opposes expanding government-funded healthcare programs beyond existing Medicare and Medicaid.
positions.notable_quote: We have a chronic disease pandemic in America linked to ultra-processed foods and chemicals.

positions.summary: Sheets strongly supports border security and enthusiastically backs the Trump administration's immigration enforcement efforts. He favors a federal E-Verify mandate for all Texas employment and supports verified labor systems to address illegal immigration in agriculture.
positions.notable_quote: Nate Sheets will enthusiastically support the Trump Administration in its efforts to secure our border and stop the flow of illegal immigrants into our state and nation.

positions.summary: Sheets is an ardent Second Amendment supporter endorsed by Gun Owners of America and Texas Gun Rights. He pledges to use the Department of Agriculture to promote and champion Second Amendment rights wherever possible.
positions.notable_quote: Nate Sheets will use the power of the Department of Agriculture to promote and champion our 2nd Amendment rights wherever possible.

positions.summary: Sheets supports natural gas and nuclear as primary electricity sources and rejects climate change urgency, describing climate policy as a justification for 'more government control.' He opposes farmers selling land to solar and wind farms, favoring agricultural preservation over renewable energy development.
positions.notable_quote: Climate change [is used as] justification for more government control.

positions.summary: No specific positions on infrastructure or transportation were found. This topic is largely outside the scope of the Agriculture Commissioner's office.

Sheets wants to help farmers and ranchers stay on their land and avoid pressure to sell to data centers, solar farms, or wind farms. He supports regenerative ranching and agricultural land preservation as core priorities of the Agriculture Commissioner's office.
positions.notable_quote: The greatest thing we can do to help rural Texas is to help farmers and ranchers be able to stay in business.

positions.summary: Sheets is focused on rural Texas and supporting agricultural communities. His emphasis on rural job creation, local food systems, and keeping farms in family hands reflects a pro-rural, growth-cautious stance for agricultural communities.

Sheets supports parental rights in education, opposes foreign influence in schools, and his campaign coalition explicitly includes the homeschool community. His opposition to gender transition for minors reflects a strong parental rights posture.
Sheets ran explicitly as a transparency and accountability reformer against an ethics-violating incumbent, pledging full department audits, zero-based budgeting, and a cleanup of the agency.
positions.notable_quote: We're going to fire every one of the cancerous people that were pro-Sid.

profiles.bio_summary: Clayton Tucker is a Central Texas rancher, former water researcher, and former kindergarten teacher from Lampasas County. He studied international politics at Southwestern University and has been active in Texas progressive Democratic politics, serving on the State Democratic Executive Committee and founding the Texas Progressive Caucus. He runs a family ranch (RX Ranch) with cattle, goats, and donkeys, serves as Secretary of the Texas Farmers Union, and has worked with former Agriculture Commissioner Jim Hightower on agricultural advocacy.
profiles.overall_summary: Tucker is running as a progressive populist challenging the corporate consolidation of agriculture and food systems in Texas. His platform centers on breaking up agricultural monopolies, protecting Texas soil and water resources from corporate exploitation (including AI data centers), expanding rural healthcare access, and reducing the influence of large donors in the Agriculture Commissioner's office. He won the Democratic primary on March 3, 2026, and faces Republican Nate Sheets in the November 2026 general election. His campaign is small-dollar and volunteer-powered, reflecting a grassroots progressive orientation.
key_endorsements: Former TX Agriculture Commissioner Jim Hightower (donor)
profiles.campaign_finance_notes: As of July 2025, Tucker had raised over $40,000 from 300+ donors with approximately $31,854–$45,130 cash on hand. The campaign is described as 100% volunteer-powered with a grassroots small-dollar donor base. Former Agriculture Commissioner Jim Hightower contributed $1,000. Texas Ethics Commission filings would contain official totals. This is a modest fundraising haul for a statewide Texas race, consistent with Democratic underdog status in a reliably Republican statewide office.

positions.summary: Tucker emphasizes that corporate monopolies—not farmers—capture agricultural profits, and advocates for market intervention to break up concentration. His platform implies support for using government regulatory power to redistribute economic benefits toward family farms and rural communities.
No positions found on public safety or criminal justice issues. This topic is largely outside the scope of the Agriculture Commissioner's office.

No specific positions found. Tucker is a former teacher and has cited the Agriculture Commissioner's role in child nutrition programs, noting 1 in 5 Texas children experience food insecurity, but no explicit education funding stance was found.

No explicit position found. As a Democrat and member of the Texas Progressive Caucus, a pro-choice position is strongly inferred, but this is outside the Agriculture Commissioner's scope and Tucker has not addressed it publicly in the context of this race.

Tucker highlights food insecurity—noting 1 in 5 Texas children go hungry—and emphasizes the Agriculture Commissioner's role in food assistance programs. His platform to lower food costs and address monopolistic pricing in food systems aligns with expanding the social safety net.

No explicit position found. As a progressive Democrat, support for preserving or expanding Social Security and Medicare is inferred, but this topic is outside the Agriculture Commissioner's scope and Tucker has not addressed it in campaign materials.

No explicit position found. As a member of the Texas Progressive Caucus, support for LGBTQ+ rights is strongly inferred, but this issue is outside the Agriculture Commissioner's scope and Tucker has not addressed it in campaign materials.

Tucker explicitly highlights the Agriculture Commissioner's oversight of the State Office of Rural Health and points to the closure of 25+ rural Texas hospitals, warning of cascading effects on emergency services and prenatal care. He frames rural healthcare access as a core issue of the office.
No positions found on immigration. While agriculture is closely tied to immigrant labor in Texas, Tucker has not addressed immigration policy in available campaign materials.
No positions found on gun policy. This topic is outside the Agriculture Commissioner's scope and Tucker has not addressed it in campaign materials.

Tucker advocates strongly for soil conservation, regenerative agriculture, reduced chemical inputs (opposing glyphosate/RoundUp and PFAS 'forever chemicals'), and a moratorium on AI data center expansion due to water depletion and energy costs. He has cited alarming statistics on Texas topsoil loss and farmland degradation.
positions.notable_quote: The nation that destroys its soil destroys itself.

positions.summary: Tucker opposes unchecked data center expansion because of its strain on the Texas grid and water supply, a populist stance favoring protecting public resources over market-driven growth. No detailed water/grid investment plan identified.

Tucker explicitly opposes conversion of farmland to data centers and other non-agricultural development, advocates for a moratorium on data center construction on Texas farmland, and commits to working with local communities to prevent farmland loss. He notes Texas leads the nation with 1.3 million acres of farmland lost.
positions.notable_quote: So ideally I would like a moratorium just to make sure that if they have to come, they're done right.

positions.summary: Tucker's emphasis on preserving rural Texas communities, protecting local water rights from outside corporate interests, and supporting family farms reflects a strong pro-rural community character stance, though this is framed at a statewide rather than local level.
No positions found. This topic is outside the Agriculture Commissioner's scope and Tucker has not addressed it in campaign materials.

Tucker frames his campaign as a rejection of the influence of wealthy donors and lobbyists on the Agriculture Commissioner's office, and runs a grassroots volunteer-powered campaign as a demonstration of his transparency and accountability values.
positions.notable_quote: We're building a people and volunteer-powered campaign — one where people aren't just donors to the campaign, but members of the movement.

profiles.bio_summary: Austin R. Kelly is a seventh-generation Texan from Comanche County, a professional ecologist, and founder of ARK Ecological Consulting. He holds multiple degrees from Texas A&M University and is completing a PhD in Rangeland Management, with prior experience as a lecturer at Texas A&M and researcher on the USDA-funded Prairie Project focused on Hill Country and Edwards Plateau rangeland management. He secured the Libertarian Party of Texas nomination for Agriculture Commissioner unopposed at the April 2026 state convention in Abilene.
profiles.overall_summary: Kelly is a science-credentialed Libertarian challenger with no prior elected office experience, running on a platform of property rights, deregulation of agriculture, and science-based land management. His platform is distinctly focused on the core functions of the Agriculture Commissioner's office — water rights, property rights, land management, and youth agriculture programs — rather than broader partisan culture-war issues. As a third-party candidate in a statewide Texas race, he faces long electoral odds but brings genuine substantive expertise in rangeland ecology and agricultural consulting.
profiles.campaign_finance_notes: No Texas Ethics Commission filings were found in public search results. As a Libertarian candidate with a grassroots campaign and no known major donors or PAC support, campaign finance activity is likely minimal. The campaign operates primarily through the website kellyfortx.com and social media, with a personal contact email (KellyForTX@gmail.com), suggesting a low-budget operation.

positions.summary: Kelly advocates for eliminating wasteful spending within the Texas Department of Agriculture and reducing selective subsidies, consistent with a libertarian small-government fiscal approach. He supports farmers retaining more earnings and opposes government involvement in farm economics.
No positions found on this topic. Not relevant to the Agriculture Commissioner's office scope.

No positions found on K-12 or higher education funding. Kelly does actively support youth agricultural education programs (4-H, FFA) as part of his platform, but this is within the purview of the Ag Commissioner role.
No positions found on this topic. Not relevant to the Agriculture Commissioner's office scope.

As a Libertarian, Kelly is likely skeptical of government welfare programs, though he has made no explicit statements on SNAP or housing assistance. His platform opposes government dependency more broadly in the agricultural context.
No positions found on this topic. Not directly relevant to the Agriculture Commissioner's office scope.
No positions found on this topic. Libertarian candidates typically support individual freedoms including LGBTQ+ rights, but Kelly has made no explicit statements.
No positions found on this topic. Not relevant to the Agriculture Commissioner's office scope.
No positions found on this topic. Not directly relevant to the Agriculture Commissioner's office, though agricultural labor is tangentially related.
No positions found on this topic. As a Libertarian, he would typically support gun rights, but no explicit statements were found.

Kelly takes a science-based approach to land and ecological management, supporting prescribed burns, invasive brush removal, and water cycle restoration — all grounded in empirical research. He opposes regulatory overreach but supports active, data-driven conservation. This is a moderate-to-market-oriented environmental stance rather than a purely conservative anti-environment stance.
positions.notable_quote: My time in the field has taught me that nature flourishes when it has the freedom to grow, not when it is choked by overgrowth.

positions.summary: No positions found on this topic. Not directly relevant to the Agriculture Commissioner's office scope.

Kelly explicitly opposes eminent domain abuse and government interference with private land use. He supports landowners' rights to manage their property without bureaucratic interference, advocates for the 'Right to Farm,' and opposes state seizure of groundwater rights. Property rights are the central pillar of his campaign.
positions.notable_quote: Just as invasive brush creeps in year after year, choking out the native ecosystem until no pristine land remains, government power expands relentlessly. If we do not push back, it will consume everything.

positions.summary: No positions found specific to local community growth or development issues. His focus is on statewide agricultural policy.
No positions found on K-12 curriculum. Not relevant to the Agriculture Commissioner's office scope.
Kelly criticizes the incumbent and promises elimination of wasteful spending, implying accountability concerns, but offers no documented transparency, ethics, or elections proposals.

profiles.bio_summary: Bo French is a 56-year-old Fort Worth-raised, Midland-bred Republican who comes from an oil family with over 80 years in the Texas energy business. He worked as an energy trader beginning in 1994, and more recently serves as a CEO, investor, rancher, and mineral owner. He served as Chairman of the Tarrant County Republican Party from 2023 until his resignation amid controversy, and defeated incumbent Jim Wright in the May 2026 GOP primary runoff by less than one percentage point to claim the Republican nomination for Texas Railroad Commissioner.
profiles.overall_summary: French ran as a hard-right "MAGA conservative," focusing his campaign heavily on social and cultural issues — ending DEI, opposing Chinese Communist Party influence in Texas oil, and promoting U.S.-Israel energy partnerships — rather than traditional Railroad Commission regulatory topics like pipeline safety or oilfield waste. His campaign was predominantly funded by conservative oil billionaires Tim Dunn and Farris Wilks and backed by far-right organizations, while he drew condemnation from fellow Republicans including Lt. Gov. Dan Patrick for inflammatory social media posts. He will face Democrat Jon Rosenthal in the November 2026 general election.
key_endorsements: Tim Dunn and Farris Wilks (major financial backers)
profiles.campaign_finance_notes: French's approximately $2 million campaign was predominantly bankrolled by conservative oil billionaires Tim Dunn and Farris Wilks, who collectively spent roughly $1 million on his behalf through the Texas Freedom Fund for the Advancement of Justice PAC and direct contributions. As of late May 2026, French had approximately $79,000 cash on hand. Other notable donors included Miles Boldrick ($25,000), Marcia French/French oil company ($20,000), and Richard Moncrief ($15,000). Texas Ethics Commission filings would contain the full disclosure record.

positions.summary: French is endorsed by Texans for Fiscal Responsibility and aligns with small-government, anti-regulation conservative fiscal values. He opposes what he calls 'Green New Deal environmental regulations' at the Railroad Commission.

French received endorsement from the Tarrant County Law Enforcement Association and has broadly aligned with pro-law enforcement positions, though he has not made criminal justice a centerpiece of his Railroad Commission campaign.

French has not directly addressed education funding in his Railroad Commission campaign. His party alignment and endorsement from Texas Homeschool Coalition suggest skepticism toward increased public school funding.

French is endorsed by Texas Values Action and Texas Family Project, both of which are strongly anti-abortion organizations. He has expressed strong conservative family values, and one source notes his 'strongly supports anti-abortion measures.'

French has not directly addressed welfare or housing assistance in his Railroad Commission campaign. His strong conservative alignment and fiscal responsibility endorsements suggest opposition to expanded social safety nets.

French has not addressed Social Security or Medicare directly in his campaign, as these fall outside Railroad Commission jurisdiction. His conservative alignment suggests preference for reform over expansion.

French explicitly campaigns against what he characterizes as 'LGBTQ indoctrination' and pledges to eliminate DEI programs, including at the Railroad Commission. He is endorsed by Texas Values Action, which actively opposes LGBTQ protections.
positions.notable_quote: Opposes 'Islam, DEI, LGBTQ indoctrination, and the Chinese Communist Party'

positions.summary: Healthcare access is outside Railroad Commission jurisdiction and French has not addressed it directly. His conservative alignment suggests opposition to expanded government healthcare programs.

French has called for deporting 100 million people and is endorsed by Texans for Strong Borders. He has promoted the 'Great Replacement' conspiracy theory and called for denaturalization and deportation of all Muslims based on religious faith.
positions.notable_quote: The Great Replacement is real

positions.summary: French is endorsed by Texas Gun Rights, signaling strong opposition to gun restrictions and support for expansive Second Amendment rights.

French advocates for further deregulation of the oil and gas industry, opposes oilfield waste rules as 'Green New Deal environmental regulations,' and pledges to keep Texas as 'the energy powerhouse of America.' He also pledges to combat Chinese government influence in Texas oil and strengthen U.S.-Israel energy partnerships.
positions.notable_quote: He opposes oilfield waste rules as 'Green New Deal environmental regulations'

positions.summary: His toll-free-highways endorsement is no longer relevant to the revised question, and he has not directly addressed water-supply or grid investment despite the Railroad Commission's energy role. A market-first lean is inferred from his conservative alignment.

As a Railroad Commission candidate, French's land-use-adjacent positions center on balancing energy production with property rights. He supports 'fair regulations balancing production with property rights' but has not addressed broader land use policy.
This topic is not directly applicable to a statewide Railroad Commission race. French has not addressed local community growth issues.

French is endorsed by the Texas Homeschool Coalition, Texas Values Action, and American Principles Project — all of which advocate for parental rights in education and against curricula they view as ideologically progressive.
French has not addressed transparency, ethics rules, or election administration in his campaign; anti-DEI rhetoric is not a transparency position.
