profiles.bio_summary: Jon Rosenthal is a Democratic state representative from northwest Houston (House District 135) who served four terms in the Texas House (2019–2026) before giving up his seat to run for Railroad Commissioner. He holds a BS in mechanical engineering from UT Austin and spent 25+ years working in the oil and gas industry as an engineer, project manager, and subsea systems specialist. He was named "Freshman of the Year" by the Texas Legislative Study Group in 2019 and is rated one of the most progressive members of the Texas House.
profiles.overall_summary: Rosenthal is running as the sole Democrat in the 2026 Railroad Commissioner race against Republican incumbent Jim Wright. He frames his candidacy around restoring accountability and technical competence to an agency he characterizes as captured by oil and gas industry insiders. His platform centers on ending routine flaring, modernizing emissions enforcement, improving grid reliability after Winter Storm Uri, scrutinizing utility rates, and expanding orphan well plugging — all while supporting continued oil and gas production. His positions on issues outside the Railroad Commission's direct jurisdiction are inferred from his progressive legislative record in the Texas House.

positions.summary: As a progressive Democrat in the Texas House, Rosenthal consistently received failing grades (F) from Texans for Fiscal Responsibility and an 17% lifetime score on the Freedom Index, indicating support for government spending and opposition to tax cuts favored by conservatives.
No explicit positions found on criminal justice for his Railroad Commissioner campaign. As a progressive Democrat in the Texas House, he likely holds center-left views on criminal justice reform.

No explicit positions on education in his Railroad Commissioner race. His legislative record as a progressive Democrat suggests support for public school funding; received a rating from the Texas Classroom Teachers Association.

No explicit statement in his Railroad Commissioner campaign, which does not involve reproductive policy. As one of the most progressive members of the Texas House, he almost certainly opposed Texas abortion restrictions passed during his tenure.
Not directly relevant to the Railroad Commission. His progressive legislative record strongly suggests support for social safety net programs.
Not a state-level Railroad Commission issue. His progressive Democratic record implies support for protecting Social Security and Medicare.

Not relevant to the Railroad Commission. As a progressive Democrat rated among the most liberal members of the Texas House, he almost certainly opposed anti-LGBTQ+ legislation passed in Texas in recent sessions.
Not a Railroad Commission issue. His progressive legislative record suggests support for expanded healthcare access, though no specific positions are documented in this campaign.

Not a Railroad Commission issue. His campaign mentions hiring community liaisons with Spanish and Vietnamese language access, suggesting sensitivity to immigrant communities. Broader immigration positions are inferred from his progressive record.
Not a Railroad Commission issue. No explicit gun policy positions found for this campaign. His progressive Texas House record implies support for stricter gun regulations.

Rosenthal supports an 'all of the above' energy policy that maintains robust oil and gas production while eliminating routine flaring and venting, implementing modern methane leak detection, expanding orphan well plugging, and scrutinizing utility rates. He credits the IRA and Bipartisan Infrastructure Law with advancing U.S. energy goals and argues the current Railroad Commission is captured by industry insiders.
positions.notable_quote: Our energy future must be 'all of the above'—prosperous, safe, clean, and responsible to our communities. Texas can lead in energy production, create high-paying jobs, and preserve our land and resources for generations to come.

positions.summary: Rosenthal emphasizes grid reliability and infrastructure resilience, citing ongoing failures exposed by Winter Storm Uri. He supports modernizing Railroad Commission oversight of pipelines and gas infrastructure to prevent future outages.
positions.notable_quote: Every time there's a freeze warning, Texans still wonder if the grid will hold.

positions.summary: Within the Railroad Commission's jurisdiction, Rosenthal supports stricter enforcement of oil and gas operations near communities and protecting rural wells from contamination. He proposes expanding orphan well plugging with public transparency dashboards.

Not directly relevant to a statewide Railroad Commission race. His platform does include community-level impacts of oil and gas regulation, including protecting frontline neighborhoods from emissions and establishing regional community liaisons.
Not relevant to the Texas Railroad Commission. No positions found on curriculum or parental rights issues in this campaign.

Rosenthal explicitly campaigns on restoring transparency and accountability to the Railroad Commission, proposing a 24/7 public hotline with case tracking, public dashboards for well-plugging progress, and removing industry influence from regulatory decisions.
positions.notable_quote: I'm running for Texas Railroad Commissioner to bring accountability and common-sense solutions to an agency that desperately needs both.

profiles.campaign_finance_notes: Campaign finance details were not available through public search results. Texas Ethics Commission filings can be searched at ethics.state.tx.us. Transparency USA tracks his committee at transparencyusa.org/tx/candidate/jon-rosenthal. As the sole Democrat in this race, fundraising is expected to lag significantly behind Republican incumbents who have historically received large contributions from oil and gas interests — a dynamic Rosenthal is running against rhetorically.

profiles.bio_summary: Tan Parker is a Republican businessman and entrepreneur from Flower Mound, Texas, who has served in the Texas Senate (District 12) since January 2023 after 16 years in the Texas House (2007-2023). He holds degrees from the University of Dallas and the London School of Economics, with professional background in technology and private equity. He campaigns as a strongly conservative voice committed to protecting "Texas values," economic development, cybersecurity, and border security.
profiles.overall_summary: Parker is a firmly conservative incumbent with a well-documented voting record aligned with the Texas Republican mainstream. He holds strong anti-abortion, pro-gun, pro-school-choice, and border-security positions, and chairs the Senate Transportation Committee. His legislative focus in 2025 centered on capital markets reform, AI governance, human trafficking enforcement, and homeland security. The Texas Freedom Index rates him at 67-72% conservative across recent sessions, slightly below the hardline caucus but consistently to the right on social issues.

positions.summary: Parker opposes expanded government spending; he voted against HB 2's school funding increase on fiscal grounds and championed the Texas Capital Markets Package to position Texas as the national leader in business registrations and capital formation. He supports low-tax, business-friendly policy.
positions.notable_quote: Applauding the signing of SB 1057 for positioning Texas as the national leader for business registrations.

positions.summary: Parker has led legislation on human trafficking enforcement, homeland security division creation, and anti-red-flag-law measures opposing extreme risk protective orders. He supports 10-year mandatory minimums for serious firearm felonies.
positions.notable_quote: Law-abiding Texans should never fear their Second Amendment rights will be trampled.

positions.summary: Parker voted yes on the SB 2 ESA voucher program — the controlling anchor — while also supporting teacher pay raises (HB 2, SB 26) and special education funding.

Parker has a consistently and strongly anti-abortion record, voting yes on the 6-week ban (SB 8), the medication abortion ban, and criminal penalties for providers. He co-authored multiple abortion restriction bills and has been endorsed by Texas Right to Life PAC and Life PAC.
positions.notable_quote: The fight to protect every human life from abortion is the human rights issue of our time.

positions.summary: No specific statements found on welfare or food assistance programs, but his consistent opposition to expanded government spending and his fiscal conservatism strongly suggest opposition to expanded social safety net programs.

No direct state-level positions found on Social Security or Medicare; as a state legislator these fall largely outside his direct purview. His broader fiscal conservatism implies skepticism of federal entitlement expansion.

Parker voted to defund Drag Queen Story Hour events and voted yes on the parental rights omnibus bill (SB 12) restricting DEI initiatives in schools. He has voted consistently with the conservative bloc on LGBTQ+-related legislation.

No explicit healthcare access legislation identified, but Parker's voting record against government spending expansion and his strong conservative orientation suggest opposition to Medicaid expansion or government-funded healthcare programs.

Parker authored legislation to create a dedicated Texas Homeland Security Division, supported Operation Drawbridge to secure the Texas-Mexico border, and has prioritized border security as a top legislative issue in the 89th Legislature.

Parker is strongly pro-Second Amendment, endorsed by the NRA Political Victory Fund, voted against red flag laws, supports limiting gun-free zones to school-owned property, and opposes local liability insurance mandates on firearm owners.
positions.notable_quote: Law-abiding Texans should never fear their Second Amendment rights will be trampled.

positions.summary: No explicit environmental legislation authored, but Parker's homeland security bills include protecting critical energy infrastructure. His conservative fiscal stance and Texas Republican alignment suggest support for fossil fuel industries and opposition to aggressive climate regulation.

Parker chairs the Senate Transportation Committee, making infrastructure a direct area of responsibility. His focus on economic development and capital markets suggests support for infrastructure investment as an economic driver, though specific transportation bills were not identified.
No specific land use bills identified. Parker's business-friendly, pro-development orientation and economic development focus suggest he would favor reduced regulatory burdens on development.
Parker is based in Flower Mound and promotes a conservative vision of community preservation and Texas values, but no specific local growth or character bills were identified in available sources.

Parker voted yes on requiring Ten Commandments in every classroom (SB 10), restricting library materials (SB 13), and the parental rights omnibus bill limiting DEI in schools (SB 12). He also voted to support the Texas Home School Coalition's agenda.
No documented positions on local-government transparency, ethics rules, or voting access; AI-governance and capital-markets legislation do not bear on this topic's dimensions.

profiles.campaign_finance_notes: As of February 2026, Parker had approximately $1,145,769 cash on hand, $405,424 in total contributions, and $491,526 in total expenditures (per Transparency USA / Texas Ethics Commission filings). He is well-funded for a state senate race. Top industry support appears to come from business and financial sectors consistent with his capital markets legislative focus. No significant campaign finance controversies were identified.

profiles.bio_summary: Ben Bumgarner is a Republican state representative from Flower Mound, Texas, serving his second term in Texas House District 63, which covers the southern portion of Denton County including Flower Mound, Lewisville, Carrollton, Coppell, Trophy Club, and Roanoke. A firearms manufacturer and owner of Evolve Weapons Systems, he previously served on the Flower Mound Town Council including as mayor pro tem. He earned a bachelor's degree from Austin College in 2007 and first took office in January 2023 after defeating incumbent Democrat Michelle Beckley. He is now seeking a third term in 2026.
profiles.overall_summary: Bumgarner is a strongly conservative legislator who scores in the low-to-mid 80s on fiscal index and Young Conservatives of Texas ratings. His legislative record reflects orthodox Texas Republican priorities: property tax reduction, school choice (education savings accounts), border security, anti-abortion measures, parental rights in education, prohibitions on gender-affirming care for minors, and strong 2nd Amendment positions. He has a high-profile endorsement from Governor Greg Abbott and broad support from Denton County Republican officials. His campaign finance base is rooted in Republican PAC and individual donor networks typical for a suburban Dallas-area incumbent.

positions.summary: Bumgarner co-sponsored the state's largest property tax reduction and backs further homestead exemption increases and spending cuts, putting property tax relief at the center of his fiscal record.
positions.notable_quote: I am excited about the results we achieved for Texans this session, but there is more work to be done. I'm running for reelection to continue fighting for the conservative reforms the voters of Texas demand.

positions.summary: Bumgarner authored legislation to crack down on human trafficking, opposes defunding police, supports initiatives penalizing cities that reduce police budgets, and backed bail reform. He authored HB 198 improving firefighter cancer screening.

Bumgarner voted yes on the $8.5 billion public school funding package including $4.2 billion in teacher pay raises. He also supported school choice / education savings accounts that allow funding to follow students to private and homeschool options.

Bumgarner strongly opposes abortion, stating life should never be electively taken via abortion. He opposes taxpayer funding for abortion providers and supports safety reporting requirements for chemical abortion drugs.
positions.notable_quote: Life is the greatest gift from God. It should never electively be taken away via abortion.

positions.summary: Bumgarner's consistent advocacy for shrinking government size and cutting wasteful spending implies skepticism toward expanding social safety net programs, though he has not made explicit statements targeting specific welfare programs in available sources.

Bumgarner favors maintaining current Medicare and Medicaid while reducing federal deficits and wasteful spending — a moderate-conservative position of preserving current benefits without significant expansion.

Bumgarner co-authored the ban on childhood gender modification procedures, co-authored legislation prohibiting biological males from women's sports, and affirms a traditional definition of marriage. He opposes gender ideology in schools.

Bumgarner opposes government-mandated vaccinations and co-sponsored a bill prohibiting private employer vaccine mandates. He supports maintaining current Medicare/Medicaid while reducing deficits, opposing expanded government healthcare programs.

Bumgarner backed $3 billion in state border security spending, sponsored bills for state-level border enforcement measures, and supports banning Chinese Communist Party-affiliated land purchases in Texas.

As a firearms manufacturer and owner of Evolve Weapons Systems, Bumgarner is an outspoken 2nd Amendment advocate who believes gun rights shall not be infringed for law-abiding citizens and supports arming teachers in schools.

Bumgarner serves on the Environmental Regulation Committee. He opposes ESG investment criteria for public funds, aligning with skepticism of climate-driven energy policy, but no detailed environmental position statements were found.
No specific infrastructure or transportation positions were identified in available sources.

No explicit land use or development policy positions were identified in available sources, though his prior service on the Flower Mound Town Council suggests some familiarity with local land use issues.

Bumgarner's background on the Flower Mound Town Council and his platform emphasizing local control and banning taxpayer-funded lobbying suggests a pro-local-autonomy stance, but specific community character positions are not detailed.

Bumgarner co-authored the READER Act removing sexually explicit and pornographic materials from Texas schools, supports parental empowerment legislation, and backed school choice. He supports cell phone bans and stricter student discipline measures.

Bumgarner's documented plank is banning taxpayer-funded lobbying — a conservative-flavored accountability restriction on local governments — plus direct local-government experience. No documented positions on proactive publication, ethics-rule expansion, or voting access.

key_endorsements: Dallas Morning News editorial board (2024 Republican primary)
profiles.campaign_finance_notes: Bumgarner files with the Texas Ethics Commission. His campaign website is votebumgarner.com. Transparency USA tracks his contributions but detailed data was not publicly accessible at the time of research. A $500 contribution was documented in a November 2025 TEC filing. His endorsement base — including Governor Abbott, multiple congressmen, and county officials — suggests strong institutional Republican fundraising support typical for a competitive suburban Dallas incumbent. No notable campaign finance controversies were identified.

profiles.bio_summary: H. Denise Wooten is a clinical psychologist with 30+ years of private practice in Flower Mound, Texas, and a former adjunct professor at North Central Texas College. She earned her bachelor's degree from William Jewell College and a doctorate from Baylor University, and has lived in Denton County for nearly three decades. She is a recurring Democratic challenger for Texas House District 63, having run in 2022, 2024, and again in 2026, where she ran unopposed in the Democratic primary and faces incumbent Republican Ben Bumgarner in the November general election.
profiles.overall_summary: Wooten runs on a progressive platform centered on public education funding (including opposition to school vouchers/ESAs), expanded healthcare access including Medicaid expansion and mental health services, reproductive rights, and sensible gun legislation. Her background as a psychologist strongly shapes her policy emphasis on mental health, social services, and educational equity. She is a longshot challenger in a reliably Republican district (HD-63 covers Flower Mound and parts of Denton County), and her campaign financing is modest relative to the incumbent.
key_endorsements: Brandon Jones, Lewisville Mayor Pro Tem
Adam Polter, Carrollton Councilmember Place 2 At-large
profiles.campaign_finance_notes: Campaign finance data via Texas Ethics Commission (TransparencyUSA) was inaccessible during research. Wooten has run three consecutive cycles (2022, 2024, 2026) as a challenger in a heavily Republican district; fundraising is expected to be modest. No significant PAC or large-donor contributions were identified in available sources.

positions.summary: Wooten supports reducing the property tax burden on middle-class Texans and advocates redirecting state surplus (Rainy Day Fund) toward education and services. She has criticized Texas ranking 45th in per capita expenditures on health, education, and welfare, implying support for increased state spending.
positions.notable_quote: Texas ranks 45th out of 50 states in per capita expenditures on health, education, welfare and civic infrastructure.

positions.summary: No explicit criminal justice reform positions identified. Her progressive Democratic orientation and psychology background suggest interest in rehabilitation-focused approaches, but no direct statements were found.

Wooten strongly supports increased public school funding tied to inflation, opposes ESA vouchers, advocates repealing the ESA bill to restore up to $1 billion to public school coffers, and supports free community college for certifications and associate degrees.
positions.notable_quote: Increased FAIR funding to meet inflationary cost increases over the past 10 years.

positions.summary: Wooten explicitly supports preserving a woman's right to make her own reproductive choices and opposes Texas abortion restrictions, stating reproductive decisions should be made with qualified healthcare professionals.
positions.notable_quote: I support women's healthcare and reproductive rights and their right to decide what to do with their own bodies.

positions.summary: Wooten supports expanding Medicaid, increasing funding for CPS and Texas Health and Human Services, and improving access to affordable childcare and housing assistance. She frames safety net programs around empowering self-sufficiency rather than dependency.

No explicit state-level positions on Social Security or Medicare found. Her support for TRS remaining a defined-benefit pension plan for teachers and opposition to shifting retirement risk onto individuals suggests a broadly protective stance on retirement security.

Wooten supports promoting individual rights for marginalized groups and has stated that at minimum people deserve safety and tolerance regarding LGBTQ issues. She criticizes so-called parental rights legislation as an attempt by "Christian nationalists" to seize control of schools.
positions.notable_quote: While diversity would be wonderful, at minimum people deserve safety and tolerance.

positions.summary: Wooten strongly supports expanding Medicaid, making affordable healthcare and mental health services universally accessible, and increasing state funding for TRS-ActiveCare and TRS-Care. As a psychologist, mental health access is a core campaign priority.
No explicit immigration policy positions were identified in available sources. Position unknown.

Wooten supports raising the minimum age to purchase assault rifles and banning high-capacity magazines, and has received an endorsement from GunSense Voter. She explicitly campaigns on "sensible assault rifle legislation."
positions.notable_quote: I will fight to increase the age that assault rifles can be purchased and for the ban of high-capacity magazines.

positions.summary: Wooten supports addressing climate change, strengthening environmental protections, and expanding renewable energy development. She has noted that Texas is the nation's top CO2 emitter and that proper environmental protections are needed.
positions.notable_quote: Texas is the #1 emitter of CO2 in the nation, and proper environmental protections are needed to achieve a healthy environment.

positions.summary: No specific infrastructure or transportation positions identified. Her general support for increased state investment in public services suggests openness to infrastructure spending, but no direct statements found.
No explicit land use or development positions were identified in available sources.
No specific positions on local community character or growth management were identified. Her campaign focuses on state-level policy priorities.

Wooten strongly opposes state-level curriculum mandates and supports local districts selecting their own curricula and library books. She characterizes parental rights legislation as a "thinly-veiled attempt by Christian nationalists to seize control" of schools.
positions.notable_quote: thinly-veiled attempt by Christian nationalists...to seize control

positions.summary: Wooten supports fair voting rights and combating gerrymandering, and has been endorsed by the NDRC (National Democratic Redistricting Committee), suggesting a strong stance on electoral transparency and democratic accountability.

profiles.bio_summary: Andy Hopper (born February 18, 1977, Kansas City, MO) is a software engineer, small farmer, and Chief Warrant Officer in the Texas State Guard who represents HD-64 (Wise County and part of Denton County). He holds a bachelor's degree from Ohio University (1997) and a graduate degree from the University of North Texas (2000), and previously taught as an adjunct professor at UNT. He defeated incumbent Lynn Stucky in the May 2024 Republican primary runoff, then won the general election in November 2024, taking office January 14, 2025 as part of the 89th Legislature.
profiles.overall_summary: Hopper is a strongly conservative first-term state representative who self-identifies as a "Jeffersonian conservative." He earned a 94/100 rating from the Young Conservatives of Texas in the 89th Legislature and holds explicit far-right positions on most major issues including abolishing abortion, eliminating property taxes, opposing school voucher voter referenda, strong Second Amendment protections, and restricting transgender healthcare for minors. He is running for re-election in 2026 and defeated a primary challenger (Lisa McEntire) on March 3, 2026. He entered the race as an outsider defeating a long-incumbent Republican and has aligned himself closely with the Texas Freedom Caucus wing of the party.
key_endorsements: Donald Trump, President of the United States
Tan Parker, Texas State Senator District 12
LTC Allen West, Dallas County Republican Party Chairman
profiles.campaign_finance_notes: As of December 31, 2025, Hopper reported $97,956 in total contributions, $57,937 in total expenditures, and $124,469 cash on hand for the 2026 election cycle, per Texas Ethics Commission data. He is not a federal candidate so FEC filings do not apply. He defeated primary challenger Lisa McEntire on March 3, 2026, suggesting his fundraising was competitive at the state legislative level.

positions.summary: Hopper advocates for eliminating property taxes entirely and constitutionally prohibiting a state income tax. He also supports freezing state spending and ending corporate tax abatements.
positions.notable_quote: Texans should own their homes, not rent them from the government. I will work to end property taxes and make sure Texas never has an income tax.

positions.summary: Hopper emphasizes law enforcement support and border security as public safety priorities. He opposes sanctuary cities and supports strong state-level enforcement measures, though detailed criminal justice reform positions are not prominent in his platform.

Hopper supports school choice / voucher programs where funding follows the student, and voted to table an amendment that would have sent the school voucher program to voters via referendum. He also filed HB 2657 to abolish the Texas Education Agency.
positions.notable_quote: I support school choice where funding follows students.

positions.summary: Hopper opposes abortion under all circumstances, believes life begins at conception, and signed the Abolish Abortion Texas Equal Protection Pledge calling for equal legal protection for all pre-born children.
positions.notable_quote: Abortion is murder and must end in Texas.

positions.summary: Hopper has not detailed specific welfare positions, but his broader platform emphasizes limiting government's role and cutting spending. He explicitly opposes public benefits for undocumented immigrants and frames government assistance programs skeptically.

Hopper states Medicaid and Medicare should remain available in the near term, but favors phasing toward a private-only system, arguing 'government has proven to be a terrible provider of any service and healthcare is not a proper function of government.'
positions.notable_quote: Government has proven to be a terrible provider of any service and healthcare is not a proper function of government.

positions.summary: Hopper explicitly opposes gender-affirming care for minors, calling it 'child abuse,' and has pledged to fight 'radical gender ideology' in schools. He holds a binary view of gender and opposes any taxpayer funding related to gender transition.
positions.notable_quote: There are only two genders—male and female—and I will never allow taxpayer dollars to fund mutilation of children.

positions.summary: Hopper opposes vaccine mandates and supports medical freedom. He favors moving away from government-run healthcare toward private alternatives and opposes government-provided healthcare as a general principle.

Hopper supports strong border enforcement, opposes sanctuary cities, and wants to eliminate all public benefits for undocumented immigrants. He endorses state-level enforcement independent of federal action.
positions.notable_quote: Texas must defend its border and protect its people... no more handouts for those here illegally.

positions.summary: Hopper is endorsed by the NRA-PVF, Gun Owners of America, Texas Gun Rights, and Texas Rifle Association. He supports making Texas a '2nd Amendment Sanctuary' and opposes any restrictions on the right to carry.
positions.notable_quote: The Second Amendment is not up for debate in my view, and Texans must have the right to carry everywhere in Texas.

positions.summary: Hopper opposes subsidies for wind and solar energy and favors natural gas and nuclear to power Texas. He disagrees that climate change requires urgent government action and prioritizes grid reliability through fossil fuels.
positions.notable_quote: No more subsidies for failed wind and solar. Texas has natural gas to power the state affordably and independently.

positions.summary: No specific infrastructure or transportation positions were found in Hopper's campaign materials or public record. He serves on the Agriculture & Livestock and Insurance committees, not transportation.

Hopper emphasizes protecting Texas land from federal overreach and opposes squatter protections. His broader platform favors property rights and minimal government regulation over land use, consistent with conservative rural district priorities.

Hopper represents a rural/suburban district (Wise County and part of Denton County) and emphasizes local self-governance and limiting state mandates, but has not articulated specific positions on local growth management or community character issues.

Hopper opposes critical race theory and gender ideology in schools, supports parental rights in children's education and healthcare, and advocates for removing objectionable curriculum from public schools.
positions.notable_quote: I will fight to drive Critical Race Theory and radical gender ideology out of our schools.

positions.summary: Hopper supports term limits for elected officials and frames government service as temporary duty rather than a career. He supports election integrity measures including a ban on electronic voting machines, favoring paper ballots.

profiles.bio_summary: Julie Evans is a retired special education substitute teacher and longtime Denton County resident who spent over two decades volunteering in public schools before entering the classroom. Born in Corsicana to a railroad family, she is a Navy spouse, mother of three, and grandmother, and earned an Associate's degree from Navarro College with additional study at UNT. She won the March 2026 Democratic primary with roughly 54% of the vote and will face Republican incumbent Andy Hopper in the November 2026 general election for TX House District 64, which covers parts of Denton County and all of Wise County.
profiles.overall_summary: Evans is a progressive Democrat whose campaign centers on public education funding, opposition to school vouchers, expanding healthcare access, and raising the minimum wage. She frames her candidacy in sharp contrast to incumbent Andy Hopper, opposing ICE collaboration with local law enforcement and advocating for voting rights expansion. Her positions on several issues — including reproductive rights, gun policy, and LGBTQ+ rights — are implied by party affiliation and Democratic primary context but not explicitly detailed on her campaign materials. TX House District 64 leans heavily Republican, making this a significant uphill race.
key_endorsements: No named endorsements identified in research; campaign website displays endorsement logos but text was not extractable
profiles.campaign_finance_notes: No campaign finance figures were located in publicly available Texas Ethics Commission summary data. Evans is a grassroots challenger with an ActBlue donation page. No significant PAC or large donor activity was identified. Texas Ethics Commission filings exist but were not accessible through web search for specific dollar amounts.

positions.summary: Evans supports raising the minimum wage to at least $15/hour and ending tax breaks and regulatory loopholes for oil and gas companies, arguing those industries do not need extra loopholes to make billions of dollars.
positions.notable_quote: Raise the minimum wage... it's essential for keeping families out of poverty and enabling them to feed you and your family.

positions.summary: Evans has not issued detailed public safety platforms, but she opposes local law enforcement collaborating with ICE to detain community members, framing it as harmful to families and the local workforce.
positions.notable_quote: When our sheriff's department works hand in glove with ICE to yank our citizens, our neighbors, our workforce, our family and friends off of the street and put them in detention centers, that is a message that Andy Hopper approves.

positions.summary: Education is her signature issue. Evans strongly supports full public school funding, opposes voucher programs as billionaire-funded privatization schemes, advocates for free K-12 meals, and supports raising teacher pay. She has direct classroom experience as a special education substitute.
positions.notable_quote: Every child deserves a thriving, well-funded school.

positions.summary: Evans has voiced support for abortion access at Democratic candidate forums, and healthcare positions include women's reproductive rights. No detailed explicit policy statement was located.

Evans supports a higher minimum wage and affordable healthcare as core economic safety net positions; she has not made detailed public statements specifically on welfare, food assistance, or housing programs beyond these general themes.
No explicit statements found on Social Security or Medicare specifically; as a Democrat running on expanding healthcare access and protecting the ACA, a progressive position is inferred.
Evans has listed LGBTQ+ health services as part of her comprehensive healthcare platform; no explicit statements on LGBTQ+ civil rights legislation were found in available sources.

Evans strongly supports protecting and expanding the ACA, extending premium tax credits, eliminating pre-existing condition exclusions, lowering prescription drug costs, and keeping rural hospitals open. She has stated every Texan should have access to healthcare.
positions.notable_quote: Every Texan should have access to healthcare.

positions.summary: Evans supports modernizing legal pathways to citizenship and enhancing border security with technology, while strongly opposing local law enforcement cooperation with ICE. She has noted that fewer than 30% of ICE detainees have criminal records.
No explicit gun policy statements were found in available sources. A progressive position is inferred from party affiliation and the general Democratic platform context.

Evans supports diversifying Texas's power grid with solar and wind energy, providing financial incentives for renewable energy companies, and ending tax loopholes for oil and gas companies. She has expressed concern about rising utility bills from an unreliable grid.
positions.notable_quote: We are paying higher utility bills just to prop up an unreliable power grid.

positions.summary: No explicit statements on transportation or infrastructure were found in available campaign materials.
Evans has called for placing restrictions on AI data center development and evaluating the development relationships with the city of Denton, suggesting a more cautious approach to rapid growth.
Evans identifies overdevelopment and rapid AI data center growth as major concerns for Denton County and has called for development restrictions, reflecting a community-preservation orientation.

Evans has not made explicit statements on curriculum battles, but her strong opposition to vouchers and support for fully funded public schools suggests alignment with mainstream public education values rather than conservative parental-rights curriculum positions.

Evans supports fair redistricting, opposes gerrymandering and mid-decade redistricting, advocates for expanding ballot access, automatic and same-day voter registration, and combating dark money influence in politics.
positions.notable_quote: Do right and risk the consequences.

profiles.bio_summary: Andy Eads is a fifth-generation Denton County native and lifelong Flower Mound resident who earned a BBA from Howard Payne University and a Master of Public Administration from the University of North Texas. He served as Denton County Commissioner for Precinct 4 starting in 2007 before being elected County Judge in 2018 and reelected in 2022. He is running for a third term as County Judge in the November 2026 general election against Democrat Nick Pappas.
profiles.overall_summary: Eads governs as a mainstream Texas Republican county executive, with a record centered on fiscal conservatism (holding the county tax rate to its lowest since 1986), heavy investment in transportation infrastructure, and strong law enforcement support including a human trafficking unit that has made nearly 300 arrests. His public positions are focused almost entirely on county-level issues — taxes, roads, public safety, economic development, and managed growth — and he has made no documented public statements on most state or national social policy issues, consistent with his role as a county administrator rather than a legislator.
key_endorsements: Texas Governor Greg Abbott (photo association on campaign website; no formal endorsement statement found)
Denton County Republican Party (listed on party site as incumbent elected official)
profiles.campaign_finance_notes: Eads files with the Texas Ethics Commission under the committee "Friends of Andy Eads." Specific contribution and expenditure totals for the 2025-2026 cycle were not publicly accessible during research. The county's AAA bond rating and multi-hundred-million-dollar budget are frequently cited as evidence of his fiscal stewardship. No significant campaign finance controversies were found.

positions.summary: Eads has consistently voted to reduce the county property tax rate, cutting it to its lowest level since 1986. He champions low taxes as his signature issue and cites the county's AAA bond rating as proof of fiscal discipline.
positions.notable_quote: Denton County has the lowest countywide tax rate among the top 15 Texas counties.

positions.summary: Eads prioritizes law enforcement funding, raised deputy pay by 14% to aid recruitment and retention, and created a Sheriff's Office Human Trafficking Unit that has yielded nearly 300 arrests. He frames public safety spending as a core county obligation.
positions.notable_quote: Strengthening public safety [is central] to ensuring Denton County remains strong, safe and well-positioned for the future.

positions.summary: County judges in Texas have no direct jurisdiction over public school funding; no statements by Eads on K-12 or higher education funding were found.
No public statements on reproductive rights were found. Score is inferred from his Republican affiliation and the deep-red political character of Denton County.

Eads touts partnerships with nonprofits such as Communities in Schools to address community needs rather than expanding government programs, consistent with a conservative preference for private-sector solutions.
Social Security and Medicare are federal programs outside a county judge's jurisdiction; no statements found.
No public statements on LGBTQ+ issues were found. Score is inferred solely from Republican Party affiliation in a conservative Texas county.

Eads has cited expansion of county mental health services as an accomplishment, suggesting moderate support for county-level behavioral health investment, but no broader healthcare access positions were found.
No direct immigration policy statements found for Eads. The creation of a human trafficking unit could touch immigration-adjacent issues but was framed solely as a public safety initiative.
No specific gun policy statements found. Score is inferred from Republican affiliation in a rural-suburban Texas county.

Eads has used ARPA funds to purchase 60 acres of natural habitat (Briarwood Retreat Center) for open-space preservation, suggesting some environmental stewardship interest, but his overall posture aligns with pro-development Texas Republicanism.

Eads has an extensive record of championing public road and transit infrastructure investment in Denton County, suggesting comfort with government-led infrastructure spending, though he has not specifically addressed state water-supply or grid investment.
positions.notable_quote: Growth that outpaces infrastructure only brings frustration. We aim to stay ahead of the curve.

positions.summary: Eads actively promotes economic development and master-planned communities in unincorporated Denton County (Furst Ranch, Landmark, Hunter Ranch) while also emphasizing managed, infrastructure-paced growth. He is pro-growth but frames it as deliberate and conservative.

Eads pairs rural-heritage preservation and open-space purchases with active business attraction and welcoming of population growth; on the growth-cost question he leans toward accommodating growth rather than strict developer-pays skepticism.
positions.notable_quote: We must never lose sight of what makes Denton County special: a sense of community, a respect for our rural heritage.

positions.summary: Curriculum and parental rights issues fall under school board and state jurisdiction, not the county judge. No statements found.

Eads has refused for four years to implement countywide Election Day vote centers — available in 99 other Texas counties — insisting on party consent the Texas Secretary of State confirmed is not legally required. His transparency efforts (podcast, statutorily required budget publication) are communication and compliance, not expanded-access policy.

profiles.bio_summary: Nick Pappas is a retired, disabled U.S. Marine Corps veteran who served from 2005 to 2022, including multiple combat deployments. A father and self-described community leader based in Denton County, he advanced from the Democratic primary on March 3, 2026, and faces incumbent Republican Andy Eads in the November 3, 2026 general election for Denton County Judge. His campaign centers on his military background and emphasizes discipline, accountability, and public service.
profiles.overall_summary: Pappas runs as a progressive Democrat in a heavily Republican county, emphasizing infrastructure investment, emergency preparedness, food security, housing assistance for the unhoused, evidence-based jail reentry programs, and transparent election administration. At a UNT-area Democratic forum in October 2025, he explicitly described himself as "pro humane immigration reform, pro women and LGBTQ rights, pro gun and pro background checks," giving unusually clear signals on social issues for a county-level candidate. Most of his positions on federal and state-level issues can only be inferred from party affiliation, as the county judge role does not directly govern those areas.
profiles.campaign_finance_notes: Campaign fundraises via ActBlue (secure.actblue.com/donate/pappasfordentoncounty). Denton County judge candidates file campaign finance reports with the local filing authority (Denton County Elections Office), not the Texas Ethics Commission. No specific filing data was publicly accessible during research. No notable finance-related news or controversies were found.

positions.summary: At a Democratic candidate forum, Pappas proposed 'taxing the rich' and a $25 minimum wage as economic priorities. At the county level, his platform emphasizes data-driven, efficient allocation of public resources rather than explicit tax positions.
positions.notable_quote: Taxing the rich

positions.summary: Pappas supports improving public safety staffing with real training and stable funding, expanding evidence-based jail programs, and improving reentry coordination for those leaving incarceration. His approach is reform-oriented and data-driven rather than purely punitive.

Education ranked among his stated top priorities at a Democratic forum. He spoke about reforming education through executive action and aligning resources with student needs, though specific county-level education funding positions are limited since county judges have limited direct education authority.

He described himself as 'pro women' at a Democratic candidate forum in the context of reproductive and bodily autonomy rights. No explicit statement on abortion policy was found, but his party affiliation and self-identification as pro-women strongly imply a pro-choice position.
positions.notable_quote: Pro humane immigration reform, pro women and LGBTQ rights, pro gun and pro background checks

positions.summary: Pappas explicitly supports strengthening food security initiatives, expanding shelter capacity for unhoused residents, and partnering with Tarrant Area Food Bank North and local mental health providers to connect families with services through data-driven resource allocation.
No specific positions on Social Security or Medicare were found. These are federal programs outside the scope of a county judge role. Position inferred from Democratic party affiliation.
Pappas explicitly stated he is 'pro LGBTQ rights' at a public campaign forum. This is one of the clearest direct statements he made on any social issue.
positions.notable_quote: Pro humane immigration reform, pro women and LGBTQ rights, pro gun and pro background checks

positions.summary: His platform includes partnering with local mental health providers and connecting residents to health services. His broader platform (at the forum) suggested support for expanded healthcare access, consistent with his Democratic affiliation.

Pappas explicitly described himself as 'pro humane immigration reform' at a Democratic candidate forum, indicating support for a more compassionate and less enforcement-focused approach to immigration policy.
positions.notable_quote: Pro humane immigration reform

positions.summary: Pappas described himself as both 'pro gun' and 'pro background checks,' indicating a moderate position that supports Second Amendment rights while favoring expanded background check requirements — a nuanced stance that is somewhat centrist on this issue.
positions.notable_quote: Pro gun and pro background checks

positions.summary: No specific environmental positions were found in his county-level platform or public statements. Position inferred from Democratic party affiliation. County judge has limited direct authority over energy or environmental policy.

Infrastructure is a central pillar of his county platform. He explicitly pledges to fix transportation, drainage, and mobility gaps using state-recommended standards, and frames strong infrastructure as essential to EMS response times and quality of life for families and businesses.

His platform includes addressing drainage and transportation gaps, which are closely tied to land use planning. He emphasizes that infrastructure systems must 'match the pace of county growth,' implying support for managed, planned development rather than unconstrained sprawl.

Pappas emphasizes that Denton County's infrastructure and services should keep pace with rapid growth, and that every resident deserves equal access to county resources. His focus on data-driven resource allocation and transparent governance reflects a progressive vision for managing growth equitably.

positions.topic_name: Education Curriculum & Parental Rights (LISD / school board)
positions.summary: No specific statements on curriculum or parental rights in local schools were found. County judge has no direct authority over school curriculum. Position inferred from Democratic party affiliation and his general pro-LGBTQ and pro-women stances.

Pappas explicitly pledges transparent communication, public explanation of all polling and administrative decisions, published election testing and audit information, protection of nonpartisan election staff, and standardized polling-place procedures — documented expanded-transparency and voting-access commitments.
positions.notable_quote: Discipline, accountability, and putting others first

profiles.bio_summary: David Wylie, 62, is a systems architect and longtime Republican activist from Argyle, Texas. He has served as a Republican Precinct Chair and Election Judge since 1990, on the State Republican Executive Committee from 2020 to 2025, and as President of the Argyle Municipal Development District since 2023. He narrowly won the GOP primary runoff for Denton County Commissioner Precinct 4 in May 2026, defeating Valerie Roehrs by just 31 votes, and will face Democrat Stephanie Draper in the November 2026 general election.
profiles.overall_summary: Wylie runs on a strongly conservative platform centered on road infrastructure, limiting government growth, election integrity, and opposing what he characterizes as threats to the community's "way of life" — including explicit statements about resisting Islamic development. His campaign attracted significant funding from real estate and land development executives, raising questions about developer influence, even as he positioned himself as a check on overdevelopment. He spent roughly $117,000, far less than his primary runoff opponent's $617,000, and won with the backing of key Denton County Republican figures including County Judge Andy Eads.
profiles.campaign_finance_notes: Wylie reported total contributions of approximately $69,186 and political expenditures of $77,275 through the runoff period, with total campaign spending around $117,315. Major donors include Tommy Cansler (CEO of TCCI Land Development, $25,000) and Terrance Jobe (CEO of ADI Communities, $25,000), both development-linked figures — notable given Wylie's stated opposition to overdevelopment. In 2018, the Texas Ethics Commission assessed Wylie $2,500 in civil penalties for failing to file required campaign finance reports tied to a 2016 Tarrant County Republican Party chair race; Wylie states he paid all fines promptly upon notification. Texas Ethics Commission filings apply as this is a Texas local race. Data sourced from KERA News reporting, May 2026.

positions.summary: Wylie advocates for limited government that delivers only essential services — public safety, roads, health, elections, courts, and tax collection — and has pledged a budget review aimed at reducing tax spending. He opposes expanding county government into areas better managed by cities or the state.
positions.notable_quote: Budget review to reduce tax spending

positions.summary: Wylie pledges full support for police, fire, and EMS and specifically supports Sheriff Tracy Murphree's 287-G immigration enforcement program. He also wants to expand county human trafficking prevention efforts.
positions.notable_quote: Support Sheriff Tracy Murphree's 287-G program implementation to reduce jail burden

positions.summary: No positions found on education funding; this is primarily a state and school district issue and was not addressed in Wylie's county commissioner campaign materials.

No explicit statements found on reproductive rights. As a strongly conservative Republican running in Denton County, a position opposing abortion access is strongly inferred by party affiliation and context, though this issue is outside county commissioner jurisdiction.

Wylie's platform emphasizes limited government focused on core services and opposes expansion into areas beyond county purview, suggesting skepticism toward expanded social safety net programs. He does volunteer with Meals on Wheels.
No positions found on Social Security or Medicare; these are federal programs outside county commissioner jurisdiction and were not addressed in campaign materials.

No explicit statements found on LGBTQ+ issues. His strong emphasis on preserving community 'way of life' against external threats and his deep alignment with the conservative Republican base strongly implies opposition to LGBTQ+ rights expansions.

No explicit healthcare access positions found beyond including county health services in his list of core essential government functions. His broader limited-government philosophy implies skepticism toward expanded public healthcare programs.

Wylie explicitly supports Sheriff Murphree's 287-G program, which allows local law enforcement to assist with federal immigration enforcement. He also made explicit statements about opposing 'the Islamization of Texas' and resisting developments associated with Islamic communities.
positions.notable_quote: The threat of the Islamization of Texas

positions.summary: No explicit gun policy statements found. His strongly conservative profile and Republican Party alignment strongly suggest a pro-Second Amendment position, though this issue was not addressed in county commissioner campaign materials.

No explicit energy or environment policy positions found. His emphasis on limited government and economic development, combined with his conservative Republican affiliation, implies skepticism toward environmental regulation.

Road infrastructure is Wylie's self-described top priority. He has pledged to work with TxDOT to accelerate road construction in Precinct 4, noting that no major road projects have begun in Southwest Denton County over the past decade despite rapid population growth.
positions.notable_quote: No major road projects have begun in this area over the past decade

positions.summary: Wylie pledges to prevent high-density overdevelopment and preserve Southwest Denton County's character, stating a willingness to 'say no to developers.' However, his two largest campaign donors are real estate developers, raising questions about the strength of that commitment.
positions.notable_quote: Protecting against developments that want to change our way of life requires a willingness to say 'no.'

positions.summary: Wylie's campaign is explicitly oriented around preserving the existing character of Southwest Denton County against rapid growth and what he characterizes as outside cultural threats. He references President Trump as a model for 'fighting without compromise' on these community values issues.
positions.notable_quote: The chaos that plagues big cities...are things we in Denton County want to keep out

positions.summary: No explicit positions found on school curriculum or parental rights as these are primarily LISD/school board matters outside county commissioner jurisdiction. His strong conservative alignment implies support for parental rights positions.

Wylie emphasizes election integrity and the importance of county government staying in its lane rather than expanding. His 2018 Texas Ethics Commission fine for failing to file campaign reports is a transparency blemish, though he claims prompt resolution upon notification.
positions.notable_quote: Candidates should file reports so the voters know what is going on

profiles.bio_summary: Stephanie Draper is an 18-year Denton County resident with over 30 years of self-described executive leadership and public administration experience. She works as an insurance agent, benefit specialist, small business owner, wedding and event planner, and hosts the "Let's Talk About It, Steph" podcast. She is married to a retired veteran, is the mother of three including an active-duty service member, has served as a precinct chair and election judge in Denton County, and has been active in the Denton County Democratic Party as a volunteer, fundraiser, and voter registration organizer.
profiles.overall_summary: Draper is running as the sole Democratic candidate for Denton County Commissioner Precinct 4 in the November 3, 2026 general election, where she will face Republican David Wylie. Her platform centers on responsive and transparent county governance, responsible growth management and infrastructure investment, expanded public transit, accessible election voting centers, and community-focused spending priorities including mental health resources and small business support. As a Democrat in a heavily Republican county, she faces a significant structural disadvantage, and her digital footprint on specific policy positions beyond local county issues is limited.
key_endorsements: Black Coalition (as listed on Blue Voter Guide)
profiles.campaign_finance_notes: No specific campaign finance figures were found in publicly available sources at the time of research. Draper uses ActBlue for fundraising (secure.actblue.com/donate/draper4texas). Texas Ethics Commission filings may contain additional data but were not retrievable directly. No campaign finance controversies involving Draper were identified; finance-related coverage in this race was focused on Republican candidates Wylie and Roehrs.

positions.summary: Draper supports transparent budgeting and protecting taxpayers from unnecessary tax increases, while also prioritizing investment in infrastructure, health services, and public safety. She advocates for small business grants and first-time homeowner grants funded through county collaboration.
positions.notable_quote: Residents deserve to know how their tax dollars are being spent and how decisions are being made.

positions.summary: Draper is committed to improving emergency response times by clarifying first responder jurisdiction issues and improving interdepartmental communication. No explicit positions on criminal justice reform were found.
No explicit positions on education funding were found. County commissioners in Texas have limited direct authority over school funding, which is primarily a state and school district matter.
No explicit statements found on reproductive rights. As a Democrat running in Texas in 2026, a progressive position is inferred but not confirmed from available sources.

Draper has described herself as a civil rights activist with a commitment to homeless advocacy and domestic violence survivor support. She supports county investment in historically overlooked communities and working families.
No positions found. Social Security and Medicare are federal programs outside the scope of a county commissioner race; this topic is not relevant to the office sought.
No explicit statements found on LGBTQ+ rights. A supportive position is inferred based on Democratic Party affiliation, but nothing was confirmed from available sources.
Draper explicitly lists mental health resources and facilities as a county government priority. She emphasizes people-first investment in health services at the county level.
No positions found on immigration. County commissioners in Texas have limited direct authority over immigration policy.
No explicit positions found on gun policy. This issue is largely outside the scope of a county commissioner role.

No explicit environmental positions were found in available sources. A more progressive stance is inferred from party affiliation, but nothing specific to county-level environmental policy was identified.

Infrastructure and transportation are central to Draper's platform. She supports road repairs guided by community data, expanded public transit connecting students to UNT and TWU, and ensuring infrastructure investment keeps pace with rapid county growth.
positions.notable_quote: Denton County is growing fast, but growth must be planned responsibly.

positions.summary: Draper supports balanced, responsible growth through collaboration with community leaders, small businesses, hospitals, and nonprofits. She has not staked out a specific anti-development or pro-development position but emphasizes community input in growth decisions.
Draper's documented platform addresses representation, town halls, and constituent access, not growth management or who should pay for development infrastructure.
No positions found. County commissioners do not oversee school curriculum; this topic is not within the scope of the office she is seeking.

Transparency and accountability are among Draper's core campaign themes. She advocates for countywide Election Day Voting Centers, transparent budget reporting, fair treatment of county employees, and regular town halls to ensure residents have direct access to their commissioner.
positions.notable_quote: County government should be responsive, accountable, and forward-thinking.

profiles.bio_summary: Paul Johnson has served as Denton County's elected Criminal District Attorney since 2007 and is running for what would be his fifth term in 2026. He earned a B.S. in Criminal Justice from UT Arlington and a law degree from the University of Houston, worked as a prison guard to fund his education, and spent 15 years as an assistant DA before being elected to lead the office. He resides in Highland Village with his wife Katherine.
profiles.overall_summary: Johnson is a long-tenured Republican DA who blends traditional tough-on-crime prosecution with pragmatic alternatives for first-time, non-violent offenders and veterans. His office has expanded pre-trial diversion, elevated human trafficking enforcement, and championed treatment courts for veterans and individuals with mental illness. His positions on most county-level issues outside of criminal justice are either not publicly stated or not within the scope of his office, making scores on many topics inferred from party affiliation alone.
key_endorsements: No specific endorsements identified in available sources for the 2026 race. Johnson filed as Republican candidate on 11/08/2025 and advanced from the March 3, 2026 Republican primary.
profiles.campaign_finance_notes: Specific campaign finance figures were not retrievable from available public sources during this research. Records would be on file with the Texas Ethics Commission (ethics.state.tx.us). Johnson ran essentially uncontested in 2022 after a 2018 primary challenge from Brent Bowen. No major donor information or spending totals were publicly surfaced in news coverage.

positions.summary: No direct statements on fiscal policy found. As a long-serving Republican officeholder, a fiscally conservative lean is inferred from party affiliation.

Johnson pursues aggressive prosecution of violent and habitual offenders while supporting pre-trial diversion and treatment courts for first-time, non-violent offenders, veterans, and those with mental illness. He has prioritized fentanyl trafficking and human trafficking enforcement.
positions.notable_quote: When you take your oath of office as DA, you are saying, 'What the law is, I will follow it, and I will enforce it.'

positions.summary: No statements found on education funding. Outside the scope of the DA's office.
No direct public statements found on abortion or reproductive rights. As a Republican DA in a conservative county, a restrictive position is inferred from party context.
No direct statements found. A skeptical stance toward expanded government welfare programs is inferred from Republican affiliation.
No statements found; outside the scope of a county DA's office.
No direct statements found on LGBTQ+ rights. A conservative position is inferred from party affiliation in a Republican-dominated county.

No direct statements found on healthcare access. His support for mental health treatment courts and diversion programs for people with mental illness reflects engagement with behavioral health access within a criminal justice context.

No direct statements on immigration policy found. Johnson has noted I-35 corridor fentanyl trafficking as a concern but made no explicit immigration enforcement statements. Conservative position inferred from party.
No direct statements on gun policy found. As a Republican DA in Texas, a pro-Second Amendment position is inferred from party and regional context.
No statements found; outside the scope of the DA's office. Conservative position inferred from party affiliation.
No statements found; outside the scope of the DA's office.
No statements found; outside the scope of the DA's office.

Johnson has noted Denton County's rapid population growth as a challenge driving increased caseloads and demand for more prosecutors and grand juries, but has not taken explicit positions on growth management.

positions.topic_name: Education Curriculum & Parental Rights (LISD / school board)
positions.summary: No direct statements found. Outside the scope of the DA's office. Conservative position inferred from party affiliation.
Long-tenured incumbent who touts ethical prosecution and lacks major ethics controversies, but has no documented position on expanded transparency, ethics rules, or election administration.
positions.notable_quote: not to convict, but to see that justice is done

profiles.bio_summary: Juli Luke has served as Denton County Clerk since 2015, winning four consecutive elections (2014, 2018, 2022, and the 2026 Republican primary). Before becoming clerk, she worked in a school district and then joined the Denton County Clerk's Office, developing a passion for public service and records management. She is running for a fifth term in the November 2026 general election against Democrat Dale Frey.
profiles.overall_summary: As a county clerk, Juli Luke's public focus is almost entirely on the administrative functions of her office — records management, transparency, online services, and fraud protection — rather than partisan policy issues. She has been a vocal advocate for government transparency, expanded digital access to public records, and property fraud alerts. The County Clerk role is largely non-partisan in practice; the vast majority of the 16-topic issue taxonomy is not applicable or addressable from this office, and her public statements reflect an administrative, service-oriented philosophy rather than ideological positioning on most topics.
profiles.campaign_finance_notes: No specific campaign finance data was found in Texas Ethics Commission online records. As a local filer, Juli Luke's campaign finance reports would be filed with the local filing authority (Denton County). No dedicated campaign website was identified; her primary campaign presence is a Facebook page at facebook.com/julilukeforcountyclerk. No major donor disclosures or notable fundraising figures were located in available sources.

positions.summary: No public statements found on fiscal policy or tax issues. The County Clerk role does not directly involve taxing or budget authority.
No public statements found on public safety or criminal justice. Her office maintains misdemeanor and civil court records, but she has not commented on criminal justice policy.
No public statements found on education funding. Her prior employment in a school district has not translated into public policy positions on education.
No public statements found on reproductive rights. This issue falls outside the scope of the County Clerk office.
No public statements found on welfare, food assistance, or housing assistance. This issue falls outside the scope of the County Clerk office.

No public statements found on energy or environmental policy. This issue falls outside the scope of the County Clerk office.
No public statements found on infrastructure or transportation. This issue falls outside the scope of the County Clerk office.

No direct policy positions found on land use. Her office records real property documents and processes related filings, and she has implemented a property fraud alert system to protect residents from fraudulent deed filings.
No public statements found on community character or growth management. This issue falls outside the stated scope of the County Clerk office.
No public statements found on education curriculum or parental rights. This issue falls outside the scope of the County Clerk office.

Luke's documented record expands transparency: online court/public records access via re:SearchTX, a property fraud alert program, and same-day open-records responses well ahead of the 10-day legal deadline. She has no documented position on countywide vote centers (elections are run by the county elections administrator, not the clerk).
positions.notable_quote: Transparency and accountability are uniquely intertwined. You can't have one without the other.

profiles.bio_summary: Dale Frey is a government web developer with experience at federal agencies including the Department of Education, GSA, U.S. Geological Survey, and NOAA. He holds a degree from George Mason University and relocated to Denton County, Texas with his wife to be near her family. He serves as President of the Frisco Democratic Club, Precinct Chair 2085 for the Denton County Democrats, and co-hosts the "Let's Talk About It, Steph" podcast. He previously ran unsuccessfully for Texas State Senate District 30 in the 2024 general election.
profiles.overall_summary: Frey is the Democratic challenger facing Republican incumbent Juli Luke in the November 2026 general election for Denton County Clerk. His campaign focuses primarily on voting access — specifically advocating for countywide voting centers on Election Day, which he argues would eliminate voter redirection he characterizes as suppression. His campaign website is thin on clerk-specific administrative policy and leans heavily on broader Democratic issue positions from his prior State Senate campaign. His digital footprint for this race is limited, with almost no independent news coverage or detailed policy positions specific to the clerk's office.
profiles.campaign_finance_notes: No specific campaign finance data was found for Frey's 2026 County Clerk race. Texas Ethics Commission filings for local candidates are searchable at ethics.state.tx.us but no figures were publicly surfaced in available sources. As the Democratic nominee who ran unopposed in the primary, his fundraising profile for this local race is unknown.

positions.summary: No positions stated specific to county clerk fiscal policy. The County Clerk role has limited fiscal policy scope.
No positions found on public safety or criminal justice relevant to the county clerk role.

Frey strongly opposes school vouchers and advocates for increased public school funding using Texas's budget surplus. These positions were expressed during his prior State Senate campaign and are not directly relevant to the clerk's office.
No positions found on reproductive rights. Not relevant to the county clerk role.
No positions found on social safety net programs relevant to the county clerk role.
No positions found on Social Security or Medicare. Not relevant to the county clerk role.

Frey supports Medicaid expansion, noting Texas loses approximately $10 billion annually by not participating. This was expressed in the context of his prior State Senate campaign, not the clerk's race.
Frey has previously advocated for immigrant rights as part of his broader Democratic platform during his State Senate campaign.
No positions found on gun policy. Not directly relevant to the county clerk role.
No positions found on energy or environment. Not directly relevant to the county clerk role.
