No positions found on curriculum or parental rights relevant to the county clerk role.

Frey's signature issue is countywide Election Day vote centers, citing 11-15% of voters redirected under the precinct system, which he calls voter suppression — a documented voting-access-expansion platform squarely on 16d's access side.
positions.notable_quote: This. Is. Voter. Suppression.

profiles.bio_summary: Kevin Falconer is a North Texas native who has lived in Carrollton and Denton County for over 32 years. He holds a Bachelor's in Architecture and an MBA from Texas Tech University and is a registered architect. He served on the Carrollton City Council (2009–2015) and as Mayor of Carrollton (2017–2022) before winning the Denton County Precinct 2 Commissioner seat in November 2022 and taking office January 1, 2023.
profiles.overall_summary: Falconer is a mainstream Texas Republican county commissioner focused on fiscal conservatism (cutting tax rates, controlling spending), public safety funding, and managing rapid growth through infrastructure investment. His positions on county-level issues are well-documented; he has no public record on state or federal social issues as those fall outside his jurisdiction. He runs in a solidly Republican county and has broad support from local elected officials across Precinct 2 cities.
key_endorsements: Former Denton County Judge Mary Horn
profiles.campaign_finance_notes: Falconer files campaign finance reports with the Texas Ethics Commission as required for Denton County (population over 350,000). Specific contribution and expenditure totals were not retrieved in this research. His campaign website (votefalconer.com) solicits donations in amounts from $25 to $500. No ethics complaints or notable campaign finance controversies were found in available sources.

positions.summary: Falconer has voted with the Commissioners Court to lower the county property tax rate every year of his tenure. As Mayor of Carrollton he cut the city tax rate for six consecutive years. The FY 2025-26 rate of $0.185938 is the lowest since 1986.
positions.notable_quote: Preventative maintenance spending helps keep the county's tax rate low.

positions.summary: Falconer lists 'Strengthen Public Safety' as a top campaign priority and supports fully resourcing the Denton County Sheriff. County budgets he supported included a 14% law enforcement pay increase in FY 2023-24 and $7.35 million for public safety in FY 2024-25.
positions.notable_quote: Public safety is the most important job of elected officials.

positions.summary: No public statements found on K-12 or higher education funding at the county level. County commissioners in Texas have limited jurisdiction over school district budgets.
No statements found. As a Republican county commissioner in Texas, a conservative position is inferred by party affiliation, but this issue falls outside county commissioner jurisdiction.

No explicit statements found. The FY 2024-25 budget included an additional $1 million for the MHMR Center (mental health and intellectual disabilities services), suggesting some support for county-level human services, though the driver of that increase is unclear.
Social Security and Medicare are federal programs outside the jurisdiction of a county commissioner. No statements found.
No statements found. A conservative position is inferred by party affiliation in a solidly Republican county. This issue is largely outside county commissioner jurisdiction in Texas.

No explicit statements found on healthcare access. The county budget support for the MHMR Center suggests some engagement with county-level mental health services, but no broader healthcare position is documented.

No statements found on immigration policy. A conservative position is inferred by party affiliation. Immigration enforcement at the county level would relate to Sheriff cooperation with federal agencies, but no specific stance was documented.
No statements found on gun policy. A conservative, pro-Second Amendment position is inferred from party affiliation, but no specific county-level actions on this topic were documented.

No explicit environmental policy statements found. As a Republican commissioner in a fast-growing county, growth-oriented infrastructure development takes precedence; no environmental protection initiatives were identified.

Falconer has backed $650 million in public bonds for roads and bridges and serves on the Regional Transportation Council, indicating support for substantial public infrastructure investment, though he has not addressed water or grid policy.
positions.notable_quote: We recognize how important this reconstruction is for the area, and we're proud to have worked closely with officials from Frisco, Hackberry, and Little Elm to bring it to fruition.

positions.summary: Falconer supports managing Denton County's rapid growth (approximately 90 new residents per day) through strategic infrastructure investment and partnerships with cities and developers. No specific restrictive land-use positions documented; his architectural background informs a pro-development, infrastructure-first approach.

Falconer emphasizes partnering with local entities to plan infrastructure ahead of development and 'protecting neighborhoods' through smart growth planning. His framing treats growth as an opportunity to be managed efficiently rather than slowed or restricted.
No statements found. School curriculum is outside county commissioner jurisdiction. A conservative position is inferred by party, but no specific actions or statements were documented.

As a sitting commissioner, Falconer has for years declined to implement countywide Election Day vote centers despite the county having clear legal authority, while touting transparency and constituent responsiveness. Documented inaction on access expansion outweighs generic transparency rhetoric.
positions.notable_quote: Meeting these needs while also making sure we are transparent and efficient in how we deliver those services is important to me.

profiles.bio_summary: Candace Valenzuela was born in 1984 in El Paso, Texas, the daughter of a Mexican-American mother and African-American father who both served in the military. She experienced housing instability as a child, earned a full scholarship to Claremont McKenna College, and built a career in education and advocacy. She became the first Latina and first African-American woman elected to the Carrollton-Farmers Branch ISD school board (2017-2019), then served as President Biden's HUD Regional Administrator for the Southwest (Texas, Oklahoma, New Mexico, Louisiana, Arkansas) from March 2022 through 2025. She narrowly lost a 2020 bid for Texas's 24th Congressional District to Republican Beth Van Duyne.
profiles.overall_summary: Valenzuela is a progressive Democrat running on a platform of affordable housing access, fiscal transparency, infrastructure investment, and reducing costs for families in a fast-growing Denton County. Her extensive background in housing policy through HUD and her school board experience shape a candidacy focused on service delivery and responsible growth management. She faces incumbent Republican Kevin Falconer in the November 2026 general election in what is a heavily Republican county. Her positions on most issues must be inferred from her prior federal-level candidacy and HUD tenure, as her current campaign website was inaccessible and local issue-specific statements are thin.

positions.summary: Valenzuela has emphasized transparency in how tax dollars are spent and lowering costs for families, suggesting a preference for accountable spending over tax cuts. She has not articulated a specific anti-tax position.
positions.notable_quote: I'll work to lower costs for families... and increase transparency on how our tax dollars are being spent.

positions.summary: In her 2020 congressional run, Valenzuela supported the Justice in Policing Act, which would ban chokeholds and establish a national police misconduct database. No specific county-level criminal justice positions have been stated for 2026.

As a former school board trustee, Valenzuela has been a strong advocate for public education funding, STEM expansion, vocational training, and early childhood education. She drew on public education and HUD assistance as the institutions that helped lift her family out of homelessness.
positions.notable_quote: What helped my family get on their feet was Housing and Urban Development and public education.

positions.summary: No explicit 2026 statements found on reproductive rights. As a Democrat endorsed by progressive women's organizations in her prior race, she is expected to hold a pro-choice position, but this has not been explicitly stated in the county commissioner context.

Valenzuela's entire career and personal story center on the value of government assistance programs. As HUD Regional Administrator she worked to expand access to housing vouchers and affordable housing. In 2020 she supported automatic government assistance for households earning under $50,000.
positions.notable_quote: My story does resonate. As soon as my constituents hear my story, it's incredibly easy for them to relate.

positions.summary: No specific statements found on Social Security or Medicare in the county commissioner context. Her 2020 congressional platform supported expanding the Affordable Care Act and a public health insurance option, suggesting support for federal social insurance programs.

No explicit county-level statements found. As a progressive Democrat, support for LGBTQ+ rights is strongly implied by her party affiliation and alignment with progressive organizations, but no direct statements have been made in the current race.
Valenzuela's 2026 platform explicitly includes expanding access to affordable healthcare at the county level. Her 2020 congressional run included support for a public option and expanding the ACA.
positions.notable_quote: As County Commissioner, I'll work to... expand access to housing and affordable healthcare.

positions.summary: No explicit immigration positions stated in the 2026 county race. As the daughter of mixed heritage and a former Biden appointee, she likely holds progressive positions on immigration, but county commissioners have limited jurisdiction over this issue.

No explicit gun policy statements found in the 2026 county race context. Her Democratic affiliation and 2020 progressive platform suggest support for gun safety measures, but no direct county-level statements have been made.

No specific 2026 county-level statements on energy or environment found. During her HUD tenure she participated in climate resilience-focused housing projects, including a $52 million investment in storm-resistant, energy-efficient apartments in Port Arthur, Texas.

Valenzuela has noted that Denton County is one of the fastest-growing regions in the country and has emphasized infrastructure investment as a county priority. Her 2020 platform also included support for roads, bridges, and waterways as drivers of economic growth.

Affordable housing access is a stated campaign priority, and her HUD background gives her deep expertise in housing development policy. She has acknowledged the urgency and opportunity of Denton County's rapid growth, suggesting she would lean toward managed, inclusive development.
positions.notable_quote: Families deserve leaders who understand both the urgency and the opportunity in front of us.

positions.summary: Valenzuela frames Denton County's growth as an opportunity to build inclusive communities. Her campaign messaging around lowering costs for families and expanding housing access implies support for growth management that preserves affordability and community access.

As a former school board trustee, Valenzuela advocated for STEM education, fiscal transparency, and quality curriculum. She has been a plaintiff in litigation opposing school closures by Carrollton-Farmers Branch ISD. No direct statements on parental rights or curriculum controversies in a LISD context were found.

Fiscal transparency is a central campaign theme. Valenzuela explicitly pledges to increase transparency on how tax dollars are spent and brings a track record of challenging incumbents and entrenched institutions in both her school board race and HUD work.
positions.notable_quote: I'll work to... increase transparency on how our tax dollars are being spent.

For the 2026 Denton County Commissioner race, Texas Ethics Commission filings would apply, but specific fundraising totals for the current race were not found in available sources. The campaign website is candacefordentoncounty.com.

profiles.bio_summary: Chris Drew is a lifelong Flower Mound-area resident and Marcus High School graduate who purchased his first home in Flower Mound in 2016. He holds a Master's in Business Administration and a Bachelor's in Mechanical Engineering, and works as a registered professional engineer and program manager in the automotive industry. Before winning his council seat in 2023, Drew served on the Planning & Zoning Commission, SMARTGrowth Commission, and Capital Improvements Advisory Committee since approximately 2015.
profiles.overall_summary: Drew is a fiscally conservative, growth-cautious local official whose platform centers on three pillars: no more apartments, lower taxes, and strict adherence to Flower Mound's Master Plan. He ran unopposed in May 2026 and was re-elected with 3,271 votes, receiving a new term through 2029. His council record reflects consistent skepticism of higher-density development and support for incremental tax rate reductions; the council unanimously adopted both the FY 2025-26 budget and a marginally reduced tax rate. On most social and national policy issues, no public positions are available — typical for a nonpartisan local official in a DFW suburb.
profiles.campaign_finance_notes: Drew files campaign finance reports with the Flower Mound Town Secretary in accordance with Texas Ethics Commission schedules. Reports on file span from April 2023 through January 2026. He ran unopposed in 2026, which typically correlates with minimal campaign spending. Specific contribution and expenditure totals were not publicly summarized in available sources; full reports are available at flowermound.gov/580/Campaign-Finance-Reports.

positions.summary: Drew campaigned on lowering the property tax rate through responsible spending and opposes year-over-year tax increases. The council, with Drew participating, unanimously adopted a marginally reduced FY 2025-26 tax rate of $0.387277 per $100 assessed value.
positions.notable_quote: I support no more apartments, lower taxes and honoring the master plan.

positions.summary: No specific public safety positions have been identified in available sources. As a local councilmember he votes on police and fire budgets, but no individual statements or votes on public safety policy were found.

Education funding is primarily a school board and state matter; no statements from Drew on this topic were found. He did encourage a developer to coordinate with Lewisville ISD on a joint access easement during a temple project review.

Drew prioritizes road investment and advocates using new traffic technologies such as light synchronization to reduce congestion. He has leveraged his engineering background to focus on traffic efficiency as a core council priority.
positions.notable_quote: Reduce traffic by supporting new technologies.

positions.summary: Drew is firmly anti-apartment and anti-high-density development, making adherence to the town's Master Plan a central campaign commitment. He voted against the 2023 TIRZ creation and has dissented on individual development decisions he viewed as insufficiently justified.
positions.notable_quote: When we change our Master Plan, we tarnish its vision and destroy the long-term plan.

positions.summary: Preserving Flower Mound's low-density suburban character is Drew's defining issue; he opposes development he believes degrades quality of life and resists ad hoc zoning exceptions and developer-friendly tax financing tools, consistent with a developer-should-pay orientation.
positions.notable_quote: Development must occur and will occur, but we must grow smart.

positions.summary: No positions on LISD curriculum or parental rights issues were found. Town council does not govern school curriculum.

Drew supports voter approval processes for new town facilities and rule-bound governance under the Master Plan, but has no documented position on proactive disclosure, ethics rules, or election access.

profiles.bio_summary: Clare Harris, age 60, is a retired registered nurse originally from Wales who has lived in Flower Mound for approximately six years. She served on the Environmental Conservation Commission, the Blue Ribbon Bond Committee, and most recently as a Commissioner on both the Planning and Zoning Commission and the SMARTGrowth Commission. She won the May 2, 2026 election for Town Council Place 5 with 54.96% of the vote in a three-way race.
profiles.overall_summary: Harris ran as a nonpartisan candidate with a progressive-leaning local platform focused on environmental conservation, managed growth, fiscal responsibility, and transparency. Although the race was officially nonpartisan, she received backing from the Denton County Democratic Party and the Flower Mound Area Democrats club, while her opponent was backed by three Republican elected officials including a U.S. congressman. Her campaign emphasized protecting Flower Mound's SMARTGrowth framework from development pressure and state legislative overreach, preserving the Cross Timbers ecosystem, expanding the commercial tax base to relieve residential property taxes, and delivering infrastructure to west Flower Mound. Her positions on national social issues are unknown, as she did not address them during the local campaign.
profiles.campaign_finance_notes: A campaign finance report (COH) was filed with the Town of Flower Mound dated January 15, 2026 (document ID 49266). The PDF was not machine-readable in this review. Harris described her campaign as a grassroots effort, contrasting it with her opponent Susan Cox, who was backed by U.S. Rep. Brandon Gill and two state legislators. No large donor amounts or total contribution figures were publicly available from accessible sources.

positions.summary: Harris supports expanding the commercial tax base to reduce residential property tax burden while maintaining fiscal discipline in town spending. She emphasized 'strengthening the long-term financial strategy' as a top priority.
positions.notable_quote: Ready to serve — with fiscal responsibility and transparency.

positions.summary: Harris listed 'providing effective and efficient emergency services to residents' as a top priority, but provided no detail on criminal justice or policing philosophy.

Harris is a strong environmental advocate, prioritizing preservation of the Cross Timbers ecosystem, native tree canopy restoration, and support for the town's Tree Rebate Program. She previously served on the Environmental Conservation Commission and is a certified Master Gardener.
positions.notable_quote: SMARTGrowth enforces that vision through measurable standards that safeguard our small-town charm while allowing for thoughtful progress.

positions.summary: Harris explicitly prioritized expanding facilities in west Flower Mound, addressing aging infrastructure elsewhere in town, and pursuing 'innovative traffic solutions.' She acknowledged the challenge of balancing new and aging infrastructure needs.

Harris strongly supports the town's SMARTGrowth framework and Master Plan as guardrails on development. She favors quality commercial development in designated areas like Denton Creek District and Lakeside while protecting the Cross Timbers Conservation Development District from overdevelopment.

Harris centers her platform on preserving Flower Mound's small-town character and defending local zoning authority against state preemption that would ease development — a growth-skeptical, character-preservation stance.
positions.notable_quote: Local decisions should be made by the people who live here and are directly accountable to our residents.

positions.summary: No positions on school curriculum or LISD matters were found. Harris's race is for town council, not school board.

Harris ran explicitly on a platform of transparency and collaborative governance. She emphasized working closely with staff, listening to residents, and maintaining accountability in town decision-making.
positions.notable_quote: The most effective council members are those who truly listen, collaborate with one another, work closely with staff, and prioritize the needs of residents.

profiles.race: Lewisville ISD Board of Trustees Place 4 / Single-Member District 5
profiles.bio_summary: Dr. Staci Barker, age 40, is a Flower Mound resident with a PhD in Educational Leadership and Policy Studies from UT Arlington. She works as a Research and Strategic Priorities Analyst at a Regional Education Service Center and has served on the LISD Board of Trustees since 2023 (Place 7). She is a parent of a student at Flower Mound High School and is a vestry clerk at Episcopal Church of the Annunciation.
profiles.overall_summary: Barker is a center-left incumbent school board member whose platform centers on whole-child education, teacher retention, student safety, and fiscal responsibility. She is endorsed by Denton Together (a progressive-leaning local organization) and was opposed by the Denton County Republican Party's endorsement of her opponent. Her language around "safe and welcoming" schools, support for diverse student programs, and a $200 expenditure to TrueBlue Democrats in 2023 all signal a progressive orientation on school climate issues, though she avoids explicit partisan language. She won re-election in May 2026 with 54% of the vote.
profiles.campaign_finance_notes: As a local school board candidate, Barker files campaign finance reports with local authorities, not the Texas Ethics Commission. The iVoterGuide profile noted a $200 expenditure to TrueBlue Democrats during her 2023 campaign cycle. No further detailed finance data was publicly accessible through state-level databases.

positions.summary: Barker touts her role in helping convert a projected district deficit into a surplus and reducing the I&S tax rate. She emphasizes responsible budgeting and transparent communication about enrollment-driven resource constraints.
positions.notable_quote: Responsible budgeting, student-centered programs and clear communication.

Barker strongly advocates for protecting and expanding student programs, preserving extracurricular and CTE opportunities, and ensuring all students have access to meaningful post-secondary pathways. She has flagged underfunding of public schools as a systemic challenge.
positions.notable_quote: Running because I love this community and believe strong public schools lift everyone.

profiles.race: Lewisville ISD Board of Trustees Place 5
profiles.bio_summary: Brian Pollard, 57, is a mortgage broker and retired educator with 30 years of classroom experience, holding a Bachelor's and Master's in Music Education from the University of North Texas and Texas A&M University-Commerce respectively, as well as a Principal's Certification from Lamar University. He is a 12-year resident of Lewisville and parent of two elementary-age students (Caiden and Charlotte) at Forest Vista Elementary within LISD. He ran for Place 5 on the LISD Board of Trustees in May 2026, losing to Dr. Staci Barker 45.93% to 54.07%.
profiles.overall_summary: Pollard ran on a platform of "Responsible Leadership = Strong Schools," emphasizing fiscal stewardship, transparent governance, and protecting academic and extracurricular programs. His campaign was driven in part by his personal experience with a campus closure affecting his family, and he consistently stressed the need for better long-term facilities planning, sustainable budgeting, and keeping students at the center of board decisions. He did not take explicit public positions on culturally charged curriculum or social issues, keeping his messaging focused on operational and financial themes typical of a school board challenger.
profiles.campaign_finance_notes: No campaign finance data was found in publicly available sources or Texas Ethics Commission search results. As a local school board candidate, Pollard would file with the Texas Ethics Commission if contributions or expenditures exceeded applicable thresholds, but no filings were surfaced during research.

positions.summary: Pollard strongly emphasized disciplined budgeting, sustainable long-term financial planning, and aligning spending with strategic priorities. He framed fiscal responsibility through the lens of both family finances and small business management.
positions.notable_quote: If it doesn't improve the student experience in the classroom, it needs to be reconsidered.

positions.summary: No specific public safety positions were found beyond a general reference to 'safe, high-quality classrooms' as a priority. No statements on criminal justice policy were identified.

Pollard emphasized protecting and maintaining academic and extracurricular programs while advocating for responsible resource allocation. He supported expanding flexible learning options such as online and blended programs, but did not advocate for increased district spending.

No positions on land use or development were found. Pollard addressed enrollment and facility changes within the district but did not speak to broader land use policy.

Pollard's focus on managing enrollment decline, thoughtful facility consolidation, and preserving the district's academic identity suggests a moderate-to-conservative preference for managed, stable growth rather than rapid expansion.

Pollard emphasized core academic basics (reading, writing, arithmetic, science, citizenship) and protecting instructional time, language that leans toward a traditional curriculum perspective. He did not address DEI, critical race theory, or specific parental rights legislation explicitly, but his framing suggests alignment with conservative educational values.
positions.notable_quote: Keep students at the center of every decision. That means supporting our teachers and principals, protecting what makes LISD strong...

positions.summary: Pollard explicitly campaigned on transparency and accountability, criticizing the district's poor communication around campus closures and demanding clear, collaborative public decision-making — a documented expanded-transparency platform.
positions.notable_quote: transparent decision-making, responsible budgeting and strong advocacy for LISD students and taxpayers
